EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of of resistant aufeis in the main channel of the Sagavanirktok (Sag) River during spring breakup forced the Sag River flow toward Milepost 403 of the Dalton Highway. The high flow from snowmelt and a heavy rain on snow event in the Brooks Range on June 10th exacerbated the impacts to the Dalton Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The main flow of the Sagavanirktok (Sag) River was diverted to the farthest west channel of the river due to aurfeis blocking the channel. The Sag River was pushed up against the Dalton Highway and began to erode the road near Milepost 403.5. On June 7th the road was closed at Milepost 403.5 due to the Sag River eroding the highway to the centerline with the threat of a total washout. This closure shut off the only road access to the oil fields on the North Slope of Alaska. The AK Department of Transportation immediately began hauling rock from a quarry 150 miles away in order to secure the road. The road reopened to one lane traffic on June 8th, although the threat of erosion continued for another week. Runoff from heavy rain and snowmelt in the headwaters of the Sag River reached Milepost 403.5 of the Dalton Highway on June 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 16, 2023, a 1002 mb low moving through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to the Bering Strait.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed on the Bering Strait coast early in the morning, of the 16th and continued into the early evening. Blizzard conditions were reported at both Brevig Mission and Wales. Brevig Mission had two distinct periods of blizzard conditions during this event, with blizzard conditions present for much of the morning, with a second shorter period in the evening. Wales reported blizzard conditions for much of the morning of the 16th. Brevig Mission reported a peak wind gust of 48 mph during the event, while the peak wind gust at Wales was 41 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front brought from 1 to 2 inches of rain to the headwaters of Birch Creek and Crooked Creek. The rain fell on a residual winter snowpack and resulted in significant runoff due to the combination of rapid snowmelt and the heavy rainfall. This caused high water and flooding along rivers and streams draining the region including Crooked Creek and Birch Creek which impacted the community of Central and portions of the Steese Highway. Flooding started in late May and continued into early June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snowmelt in the Birch Creek headwaters caused Birch Creek to flow overbank and flood portions of the Steese Highway between milepost 145 to 147. The flooding caused the highway to be closed for a period of time due to dangerous driving conditions. Birch Creek fell back within bank on June 2nd.

CZ_NAME: WRN TANANA VLY WRN YUKON VLY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of well-below-average spring temperatures combined with an above-average winter snowpack created a dynamic breakup of the Yukon River. This resulted in numerous ice jams and snowmelt flooding on the Yukon River. Breakup flooding began on May 12th in eastern Alaska in Eagle and ended on June 3rd in the Yukon River delta at the Bering Sea coast. The primary cause of this active flood year was a 1-2 week delay in breakup when compared to typical timing. April temperatures were well below normal, and when temperatures began to warm in early May, it melted the low-elevation snowpack quickly. Because of the relatively quick addition of snowmelt onto strong ice, the ice was more susceptible to jamming and floods. In addition to the late breakup, the snowpack this winter was 1 to 1.5 times greater than the climatological normal for interior and western Alaska. Significant ice jams and associated flooding on the Upper Yukon River resulted in large volumes of ice and water to move down into the Middle Yukon River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Yukon River approx 100 miles downriver from Tanana caused minor flooding in the community.

CZ_NAME: S. SLOPES OF ERN BROOKS RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front brought high freezing levels and heavy rain to the Brooks Range. The combination of rainfall and snowmelt runoff from a late winter snowpack resulted in minor flooding along the Koyukuk River and tributaries draining the Brooks Range. Rainfall totals ranged from 1 to 2 inches between June 8th to the 10th, with the bulk of the rain falling in less than 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water levels rose rapidly on the Middle Fork of the Koyukuk River on Friday June 9th and went overbank and caused minor flooding by Saturday morning June 10th. At Wiseman, residents reported approximately 3 inches of water over the lower part of the access road into Wiseman. The high water caused bank erosion and some erosion on the Wiseman Road surface. High groundwater levels also caused flooding of the crawl space in at least one home in Wiseman along Wiseman Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front brought from 1 to 2 inches of rain to the headwaters of Birch Creek and Crooked Creek. The rain fell on a residual winter snowpack and resulted in significant runoff due to the combination of rapid snowmelt and the heavy rainfall. This caused high water and flooding along rivers and streams draining the region including Crooked Creek and Birch Creek which impacted the community of Central and portions of the Steese Highway. Flooding started in late May and continued into early June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on snow caused Crooked Creek in Central to rise overbank and flood portions of the community. The public reported flood waters up to the doors of the Central Museum, into the parking lot of the local store, and up to 6 inches deep over the Steese Highway in Central. Residents put sandbags around the Central Museum but water still seeped in through the back door and got the carpet and floor wet. Floodwaters crested Thursday afternoon May 31st and flooding ended by Friday morning Jun 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of deep warm, southerly flow aloft combined with synoptic frontal lift over-top air starting out below freezing at ground level combined together to produce a mess of mixed precipitation types across much of Southcentral Alaska between 1/22 and 1/25.  Warm air with southerly winds gradually was able to work north and change wet snow over to rain as it filtered into interior valleys, with some mix with freezing rain observed briefly during the transition phase in a couple locations.  While precipitation amounts were generally light, aside from over a foot of snowfall observed in parts of the Susitna Valley, the sudden thaw and rain on top of snow and cold ground resulted in significant disruptions to travel and daily life for many residents across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 6 AM 1/24, Alaska Department of Transportation reported 14 inches of new snowfall along the Parks Highway between Mile Post 121 and 163 with difficult driving conditions.  The heaviest snowfall amounts were all focused on the northern end of the Susitna Valley, with areas farther south mixing with rain as warmer air intruded northwards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of weak lift aloft and temperatures near freezing below the snow growth layer of the the atmosphere was able to facilitate a period of light freezing rain and drizzle across parts of the Kenai Peninsula, Matanuska Valley and Anchorage area during the afternoon of 1/29.  While ice accumulation was very light, it did not take much to cause significant travel disruptions, with multiple ditched cars and accidents reported along the Glenn Highway between Palmer and Anchorage on the afternoon of 1/29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Soldotna AWOS reported a mix of freezing rain and snow for a few hours during the afternoon of 1/29.  Little to no ice accumulation occurred based on reports available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of weak lift aloft and temperatures near freezing below the snow growth layer of the the atmosphere was able to facilitate a period of light freezing rain and drizzle across parts of the Kenai Peninsula, Matanuska Valley and Anchorage area during the afternoon of 1/29.  While ice accumulation was very light, it did not take much to cause significant travel disruptions, with multiple ditched cars and accidents reported along the Glenn Highway between Palmer and Anchorage on the afternoon of 1/29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Anchorage Police Department responded to a multi-vehicle crash on the inbound side of the Glenn Highway near Arctic Valley at around 4:30 PM AKST 1/29.  Officers reported very icy conditions on the Glenn Highway at the time.  A Seward Highway Facebook Group commenter mentioned very slick roadways along the same stretch of highway with multiple cars either rolled over or in ditches at around 7 PM AKST the same evening.  Freezing rain was also reported multiple times by Anchorage area mesonet sensors during the course of the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of deep warm, southerly flow aloft combined with synoptic frontal lift over-top air starting out below freezing at ground level combined together to produce a mess of mixed precipitation types across much of Southcentral Alaska between 1/22 and 1/25.  Warm air with southerly winds gradually was able to work north and change wet snow over to rain as it filtered into interior valleys, with some mix with freezing rain observed briefly during the transition phase in a couple locations.  While precipitation amounts were generally light, aside from over a foot of snowfall observed in parts of the Susitna Valley, the sudden thaw and rain on top of snow and cold ground resulted in significant disruptions to travel and daily life for many residents across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska Department of Transportation reported a myriad of mixed precipitation starting out as snow, then mostly transitioning to rain as warm air above freezing filtered into valleys across the Copper River Basin from the south with time between 1/24 and 1/25.  Most locations did not receive significant snowfall or rainfall totals (no more than 6-7 inches of new snowfall prior to transition). Tazlina reported a brief period of freezing rain before switching over from snow to all rain during at around 5 AM 1/25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal system associated with a powerful low moving south of the Kamchatka Peninsula moved north into the western Aleutian Chain out of the North Pacific on 1/28, producing strong easterly winds as it crossed over into the southern Bering Sea.  The strongest winds by far were reported at Shemya, where peak gusts to 80 mph occurred during the late afternoon and evening hours of 1/28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS sensor in Shemya began to report severe winds gusting above 75 mph around 6 PM AKST 1/28 and reported a peak gust of 82 mph at around 7 PM AKST 1/28 before winds began to decrease as the front pushed past to the north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A localized area of banded snowfall moved over Saint George with strong and gusty northeast winds in place at the surface, producing near whiteout conditions for several hours on 1/24.  This was localized enough that the snowfall never made it far enough north to really affect Saint George, and thus impact were limited to Saint George Island alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saint George AWOS reported several hours of snow, blowing snow and visibility fluctuating between 1/4 and 1/2 miles for much of the afternoon and evening of 1/24, with sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph and gusts near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up from the North Pacific past Kodiak Island and into the Kenai Peninsula on 2/5, with very strong winds over the Gulf spreading into parts of Bristol Bay and the Kenai Peninsula.  These winds combined with light snow to produce areas of blowing snow and visibility reduced down to less than a mile for several hours in a few spots, including Portage Valley, King Salmon and New Stuyahok.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Portage AWOS recorded strong easterly winds gusting to around 45 MPH with light to moderate snow and blowing snow, with visibility reduced to 1/2 mile or less between about 10 AM and 5 PM AKST 2/5.  The Chugach National Forest Avalanche Center also reported 1/2 mile visibility or less at times with 6 inches of new snowfall and wind gusting between 25 and 35 mph at 2 PM AKST the same day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up from the North Pacific past Kodiak Island and into the Kenai Peninsula on 2/5, with very strong winds over the Gulf spreading into parts of Bristol Bay and the Kenai Peninsula.  These winds combined with light snow to produce areas of blowing snow and visibility reduced down to less than a mile for several hours in a few spots, including Portage Valley, King Salmon and New Stuyahok.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The New Stuyahok and King Salmon AWOS stations both reported visibility around 1/2 mile or less from about 12 PM to 3 PM AKST 2/5 with snow, blowing snow and northeast winds gusting to near 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low moving onshore over the Yukon Delta brought a period of snow, gusty onshore winds and blowing snow along the coastal communities of portions of Southwest starting early in the morning on 3/1, with the worst conditions focused from Togiak north and west towards Mekoryuk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mekoryuk AWOS began reporting near blizzard conditions with 1/2 mile visibility or less and sustained winds around 30 to 40 mph beginning shortly before 1 AM 3/1.  Gusty west winds continued much of the day on 3/1 into the afternoon with very low visibility and lasted into the early afternoon.  Conditions at Kipnuk were similar for much of the day on 3/1 through the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of banded snowfall set up across Anchorage starting on the morning of 2/12 and lasting for more than 24 hours into the morning of 2/13.  A very deep, saturated snow growth layer with surface temperatures down in the 10s to 20s for the duration of the event resulted in very high snowfall ratios that helped boost totals to the 12 to 16 inches observed across eastern parts of town and across the Anchorage Hillside and Eagle River, where west to southwest winds sloping up higher terrain likely helped contribute to the higher totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple public and trained spotter reports of snowfall of 12 - 16 inches were received from East Anchorage, Anchorage Hillside, Eagle River through early morning 2/13.  the Anchorage International Airport also reported a record daily snowfall for 2/12 of 7.1 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low from the North Pacific swept through the western and central Aleutians on the evening of February 20 through midday on February 21, bringing high winds to the community of Adak. Storm force winds were aided by a strong (1045 mb) surface high pressure south of the Gulf of Alaska, which led to a strong pressure gradient on the east side of the low as it intensified over the western Aleutians late on February 20. The swath of strongest winds pushed through the central Aleutians near Adak during the early morning hours of February 21, before the low quickly exited into the northern Bering Sea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Ocean Service observing station in Adak recorded several wind gusts in excess of 65 knots (75 mph) during the morning hours of February 21. There was also an isolated wind gust to 69 knots (79 mph) during the previous afternoon, though winds during this secondary peak otherwise remained below the high wind threshold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front brought a deep fetch of moisture and warm air aloft over Southcentral Alaska on February 23, leading to a mixture of mostly snow, with some rain. Freezing rain/drizzle was observed in parts of the western Kenai Peninsula throughout the day as the warm air rode over top a cold air mass which remained below freezing at the surface. Precipitation tapered off and changed over to rain by the evening hours as the front began to shift east and warm air working in on southerly flow brought temperatures above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Soldotna AWOS reported unknown precipitation at a temperature of 25 degrees Fahrenheit during the morning hours of February 23. Later that day, a pilot reported light rain at Kenai Municipal Airport while temperatures remained around 26. A member of the public also reported drizzle with a trace accumulation of ice early in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Bora windstorm impacted Southcentral Thursday, March 9 through Friday night, March 10 as a shortwave trough dug southward across Southcentral Alaska, ushering Arctic air southward into the Gulf of Alaska. While areas around Southcentral experienced heightened winds, the windstorm primarily impacted the Valdez area and its vicinity, as the temperature differentiation increased and the subsequent pressure gradient differentiation heightened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Thompson Pass DOT camp reported that their highest observed wind gust was 96 mph at camp. They also reported that the wind gusts were much stronger through the Thompson Pass gap. Meanwhile, the Valdez ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough and embedded low overspread much of mainland Alaska, while a series of shortwaves rotated through the trough. At the surface, an attendant low and its front, extended from near Kodiak Island and out along the Southcentral coast. As a result, widespread (heavy) snow showers persisted along the Southcentral coastal regions and gusty winds blanketed the Gulf of Alaska, Prince William Sound, and areas north towards the Copper River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kyle Sobek (Thompson Pass DOT) reported 2 to 2 1/2 feet storm total snow at Thompson Pass and 10 in Tonsina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low moving onshore over the Yukon Delta brought a period of snow, gusty onshore winds and blowing snow along the coastal communities of portions of Southwest starting early in the morning on 3/1, with the worst conditions focused from Togiak north and west towards Mekoryuk.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kipnuk AWOS reported visibility as low as 1/4 mile and 25 to 30 mph sustained onshore winds intermittently from 9 am to 3 pm 3/1.  Similarly poor, near blizzard conditions were observed for several hours at the Port Heiden AWOS farther south along the Alaska Peninsula during about the same time frame, with 1/4 mile visibility or less at times with winds sustained at 30 to 45 mph out of the west.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled low pressure system off the coast, in the Northern Gulf, provided an optimal pressure gradient for gusty offshore flow, as it paired with high pressure center over the mainland, and a highly amplified jet stream. The combination of these factors brought high winds through Thompson Pass, and into portions of Valdez, Friday evening, March 3 through late Saturday morning March 4. Gusts to 75 mph paired with transportable snow for heavy drifting noted near the Thompson Pass gap and blowing snow reducing visibilities down to a mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reported that wind gusts reached 75 mph around 2:00 am along with heavy drifting snow in and near the Gap, while visibility reduced to 1 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded low and its associated front transitioned from Southwest Alaska under a highly amplified pattern, and propagated towards Kodiak Island producing snow and blowing snow impacts. It then spun up a triple point low with a southerly moisture tap near the southern Kenai Peninsula, producing blizzard conditions and heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Portage Glacier Center ASOS recorded 1/4 mile visibility in heavy snow and frequent gusts 35+ mph from 8:44 AM - 11:53 AM. While, visibility was reduced to 1/4 or 1/2 mile due to heavy snow in many locations, no other stations saw prolonged periods of 1/4 mile vis with winds 35+ mph. ||- Alaska DOT reported difficult driving conditions along Seward Hwy from Indian Road, all the way to Seward. Whiteout conditions described on Alyeska Highway. Atmospheric conditions described as Winter Storm from Seward Hwy at Ingram Creek to Hope Y, with very difficult conditions (Travel is not advised) for Turnagain Arm. ||- Multiple reports of whiteout conditions, vehicles in ditches along Seward Hwy road conditions Facebook groups. Several posts were made about the blizzard warning in these groups.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low and accompanying front moved northward over SE AK. Low level winds from the frontal band mixed down to generate high surface winds. Very heavy wet snow also fell over the Klondike Highway and White Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to heavy snow and increased avalanche danger, the Klondike Highway was closed at 330pm on the 20th. State DOT reported 28cm or 11 inches of snow had fallen at their Moore Creek station by 230pm with rates of 1.5 inches per hour. DOT noted 30cm (11.8 inches) at White Pass and another 10cm (3.9 inches) by the next morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low and accompanying front moved northward over SE AK. Low level winds from the frontal band mixed down to generate high surface winds. Very heavy wet snow also fell over the Klondike Highway and White Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg Seaplane Base measured a peak gust of 64 mph at 112pm on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low and accompanying front moved northward over SE AK. Low level winds from the frontal band mixed down to generate high surface winds. Very heavy wet snow also fell over the Klondike Highway and White Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marine Exchange station at the AML Dock and at their office measured a peak gust of 65 and 62 mph respectively during the same hour. During this event the Juneau Eaglecrest Ski area recorded peak gusts of 60-92 mph between 12pm and 10pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low and accompanying front moved northward over SE AK. Low level winds from the frontal band mixed down to generate high surface winds. Very heavy wet snow also fell over the Klondike Highway and White Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rooftop wind at the Ketchikan Airport measured a peak gusts of 50-70kt between 1153am and 4pm on the 20th. COOP observer to the north at 3pm noted strong SE wind of 35-50 mph with higher gusts to 60 mph estimated and tree debris all around from high wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center moved south of the Kenai Peninsula on 4/9 as its front curved northwest into the Prince William Sound.  Much cooler air and Arctic high pressure was entrenched over the southern Mainland ahead of this system, leading to a very strong pressure gradient along the Gulf Coast and strong offshore gap winds developing out of fjords and passes ahead of the low and frontal system.  Moderate snowfall moved into the Kenai Peninsula as gusty westerly winds continued through Portage Valley and Whittier, leading to nearly 24 hours of near whiteout conditions for Whittier between 4/9 and 4/10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two observation sites in Whittier began reporting west winds sustained at 30 to 35 mph gusting between 40 and 50 mph with 1/4 mile visibility or less between 6 am and 7 am AKST 4/9, lasting close to 24 hours until 6 am AKST 4/10 as winds began to diminish.  This was also supported by local webcam imagery that showed very poor visibility beginning around the same time and lasting for many hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly flow moving into the Susitna Valley with an upper level trough helped support enough upslope enhancement into terrain with the background forcing and lift to generate moderate to heavy snowfall rates across northern parts of the region from the evening of 4/11 to the morning of 4/12.  The highest new snowfall amounts of up to 1.5 feet were focused along the Parks Highway north of Talkeetna.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alaska Department of Transportation reported 18 inches of new snowfall that had fallen overnight near milepost 121 of the Parks Highway (northwest of Talkeetna) on the morning of 4/12.  A spotter in Amber Lake also reported 10.5 inches of new snowfall during the same time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over the Arctic Ocean combined with an area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea brought strong northeasterly winds and blizzard conditions to the Chukchi Sea Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at Point Lay early in the morning on the 11th and continued through much of the day on the 12th. Northeasterly winds with gusts up to 49 mph were seen with this system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure in the southern Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to the Yukon Delta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed in the Yukon Delta early in the morning on the 22nd and continue into the early morning hours on the 23rd. Cape Romanzof saw wind gusts as high as 93 mph during this event, while Chevak saw gusts as high as 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low and accompanying front moved northward over SE AK. Low level winds from the frontal band mixed down to generate high surface winds. Very heavy wet snow also fell over the Klondike Highway and White Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marine Exchange station at the Sitka Harbor measured a peak gust of 68 mph. During the same hour the airport measured a peak gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force low and accompanying front moved northward over SE AK. Low level winds from the frontal band mixed down to generate high surface winds. Very heavy wet snow also fell over the Klondike Highway and White Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marine Exchange station at Tenakee Springs measured a peak gust of 64 mph. During the same hour the nearby FAA webcam measured sustained wind of 40-45mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front associated with a low in the Bering Sea brought a mixture of snow, rain and freezing rain to the Yukon Delta and the Lower Yukon Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow transitioned to freezing rain on the evening of the 29th and eventually transitioned to rain on the morning of the 30th. The combination of freezing rain and rain falling on frozen ground caused around a tenth of an inch of ice to accumulate on the ground in Russian Mission.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded low and its associated front transitioned from Southwest Alaska under a highly amplified pattern, and propagated towards Kodiak Island producing snow and blowing snow impacts. It then spun up a triple point low with a southerly moisture tap near the southern Kenai Peninsula, producing blizzard conditions and heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Akhiok sensor recorded visibilities at or less that a half mile from 10:35 AM to 11:20 AM. A nearby sensor registered frequent gusts of 40+ mph at the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level low dropped south into the Kuskokwim Delta and eastern Bering Sea, as multiple shortwaves passed through its flow. These shortwaves pulled cold air southward, leading to increased winds in response to these features, as well as snowfall along the Alaska Peninsula and eastern Aleutian Islands as a cold front ahead of the low crossed the Alaska Peninsula and eastern Aleutians beginning Monday night, March 13. Widespread snow showers continued through Thursday, March 16, as gusty winds produced blowing snow and reduced visibilities at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty northerly winds along with snow and blowing sow were reported at the Dutch Harbor ASOS. Periods of low visibility were also recorded at the ASOS ranging from 1/2 to 1/4 sm. Snow and northwesterly winds 25-30 mph were noted, with gusts to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough and embedded low overspread much of mainland Alaska, while a series of shortwaves rotated through the trough. At the surface, an attendant low and its front, extended from near Kodiak Island and out along the Southcentral coast. As a result, widespread (heavy) snow showers persisted along the Southcentral coastal regions and gusty winds blanketed the Gulf of Alaska, Prince William Sound, and areas north towards the Copper River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kodiak City leadership reported storm total snow amounts of 12-14 inches (more than what was reported at the airport). They also mentioned that the Coast Guard Base had shut down for the day.||The Kodiak Airport ASOS noted visibilities of a quarter mile in moderate snow showers and freezing fog at the peak of the event. Westerly winds were noted at 26 kts with gusts to 40 kts at 2:12PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough and embedded low overspread much of mainland Alaska, while a series of shortwaves rotated through the trough. At the surface, an attendant low and its front, extended from near Kodiak Island and out along the Southcentral coast. As a result, widespread (heavy) snow showers persisted along the Southcentral coastal regions and gusty winds blanketed the Gulf of Alaska, Prince William Sound, and areas north towards the Copper River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reported 10 inches new snow in Tonsina. Meanwhile, the 511 webpage noted very difficult road conditions from MM 42 to 82 along the Richardson Hwy due to heavy snow, wind, blowing snow, drifting snow, icy patches, and reduced visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front associated with a low in the Bering Sea brought a mixture of snow, rain and freezing rain to the Yukon Delta and the Lower Yukon Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow transitioned to freezing rain on the morning of the 29th and eventually transitioned to rain on the morning of the 30th. The combination of freezing rain and rain falling on frozen ground caused a tenth of an inch of ice to accumulate on the frozen ground in St Marys and in Chevak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Pressure over the northern Chukchi Sea combined with trough of low pressure over the Seward Peninsula brought high winds to the Chukchi Sea Coast. Initially, the strong winds were accompanied by blowing snow resulting in blizzard conditions; however, as time passed, the visibility improved even through the winds increased in speed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed early in the morning of the 14th and continued through late morning. By late morning, the visibility improved, even though the winds remained strong.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Pressure over the northern Chukchi Sea combined with trough of low pressure over the Seward Peninsula brought high winds to the Chukchi Sea Coast. Initially, the strong winds were accompanied by blowing snow resulting in blizzard conditions; however, as time passed, the visibility improved even through the winds increased in speed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The winds gradually increased during the day on the 15th, with the peak wind gust occurring in the early evening. By late evening, the winds began to die down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Pressure over the northern Chukchi Sea combined with trough of low pressure over the Seward Peninsula brought high winds to the Chukchi Sea Coast. Initially, the strong winds were accompanied by blowing snow resulting in blizzard conditions; however, as time passed, the visibility improved even through the winds increased in speed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four hour period of blizzard conditions developed at Point Hope on the afternoon of the 17th as light snow fell in an area where strong winds had persisted for several days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Pressure over the northern Chukchi Sea combined with trough of low pressure over the Seward Peninsula brought high winds to the Chukchi Sea Coast. Initially, the strong winds were accompanied by blowing snow resulting in blizzard conditions; however, as time passed, the visibility improved even through the winds increased in speed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and blowing snow were reported across the Seward Peninsula on the 17th. Brevig Mission reported wind gusts as high as 67 mph. The strong winds caused the visibility as to one half mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of high pressure over the Beaufort Sea combined with a strong area  of low pressure in the Bering Sea caused strong easterly winds to develop over the North Slope of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at Barter Island on the morning of the 1st and lasted into early in the day on the 2nd. Barter Island saw wind gusts as high as 55 mph from the southeast during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of high pressure over the Beaufort Sea combined with a strong area  of low pressure in the Bering Sea caused strong easterly winds to develop over the North Slope of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong east winds brought blizzard conditions to Point Hope. Blizzard conditions lasted from the evening of the 1st through early in the morning of the second. Blowing snow continued during the day on the second, with several periods where the visibility dropped below one quarter mile briefly. Winds at Point Hope gusted as high as 45 mph during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low near Point Hope and high pressure north of the Arctic Coast of Alaska produced strong northeast winds and low wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Point Lay AWOS reported 35 MPH with wind chills as low as -65F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front became more convective as it moved onshore with a wide swath of storm force gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hydaburg Seaplane Base AWOS measured frequent peak gusts near or above 60 mph throughout New Years Day, with a peak of 67 knots, or 77 mph, at 544pm. The RAWS station near Thorne Bay on the opposite side of Prince of Wales Island measured a peak gust of 62 mph at 154 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low near Point Hope and high pressure north of the Arctic Coast of Alaska produced strong northeast winds and low wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Point Lay AWOS reported wind chills as low as -69F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moved over the panhandle from the west with increasing rates that caused snow to accumulate even at temperatures above freezing, then becoming mixed or changing to rain near sea level.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Retired NWS employee measured 5 inches of snow between 3pm and 750pm. Estimated another 2 inches fell before it changed to rain around midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moved over the panhandle from the west with increasing rates that caused snow to accumulate even at temperatures above freezing, then becoming mixed or changing to rain near sea level.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morning DOT Haines Highway report from 424am on the 6th state there was approximately 5 to 8 inches of snow on the road. Trained spotters from Mud Bay, south of Haines, reported a range of 6 to 11 inches fell over a 12 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low moving through the Bering Sea brought strong winds and falling snow to the West Coast, causing periods of quarter mile or less visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tin City and Brevig Mission AWOS had multiple hours of quarter mile visibility with gusts as high as 50 MPH and heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low moving through the Bering Sea brought strong winds and falling snow to the West Coast, causing periods of quarter mile or less visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Point Lay AWOS reported multiple hours of quarter mile visibility with wind gusts as high as 45 MPH and heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low moving through the Bering Sea brought strong winds and falling snow to the West Coast, causing periods of quarter mile or less visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chevak AWOS reported multiple hours of quarter-mile visibility with gusts as high as 48 MPH and heavy snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small low tracked north across the region producing quickly accumulating snow across the Juneau area followed by persistent showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Tee Harbor, north of Juneau, said that snow begin at 9pm on the 1st and measured 7.75 inches by 9am the next morning. Another 3 inches fell at their location by noon. Other locations around Juneau measured 3 to 6 inches but there were several cars off the road and a power outage in the Mendenhall Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of light snow fell across the northern panhandle over 24 hours as a low pressure system tracked into the SE gulf from the west. Then a deformation band became more organized and set up north to south from Haines to Hoonah with a shorter period of heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT estimated 6 to 10 inches of new snow on the Haines Highway at 424am the morning of the 10th. A trained spotter on Mud Bay, south of Haines, reported that around 2 inches of snow fell during the day on the 9th, then became heavy around 8pm with a total of 9.5 inches falling by the morning. Another trained spotter in town measured 9 inches of dense snow had fallen by 1230am, which was in addition to 2 inches he had cleared during the day, and was still falling moderately at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moving north through the Bering Sea brought strong winds and falling snow, causing low visibility and low wind chills to the Bering Strait and Chukchi Sea Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tin City AWOS reported more than 3 hours of quarter mile visibility with winds gusting as high as 56mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moving north through the Bering Sea brought strong winds and falling snow, causing low visibility and low wind chills to the Bering Strait and Chukchi Sea Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Point Lay AWOS more than 3 hours of quarter mile visibility with winds gusting to 39 MPH and wind chills as low as -65F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new low center developed along a triple point frontal system causing enhancement as the front moved over the region with heavy snow and blowing snow late on the 14th through the morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer in Hyder measured 2.8 inches of snow early on the 15th and another 12.6 inches had fallen by the following morning. Nearby Stewart B.C. observation site measured over 9 inches of snow by 4pm AKST on the 15th and was still snowing moderately.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new low center developed along a triple point frontal system causing enhancement as the front moved over the region with heavy snow and blowing snow late on the 14th through the morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple spotters in Haines and south along the Chilkat Peninsula reported heavy snow, including 6 to 12 inches measured in the first 12 hours of the event and storm totals ranging from 11 to 17 inches. Much lighter amounts were observed up the Haines Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new low center developed along a triple point frontal system causing enhancement as the front moved over the region with heavy snow and blowing snow late on the 14th through the morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple spotters in the Juneau Mendenhall Valley and on Douglas Island reported heavy snow, including around 8 inches measured by 8 am on the 15th. Storm totals ranged from 6 to 14 inches. Lower amounts were measured near Lena Point and out the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new low center developed along a triple point frontal system causing enhancement as the front moved over the region with heavy snow and blowing snow late on the 14th through the morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer in Hoonah measured 5.8 inches of new snow at 8am on the 15th and was still snowing lightly. Two reports from Facebook included noted a total of 8 inches in Hoonah with one including 4 inches of that falling between 3-7am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new low center developed along a triple point frontal system causing enhancement as the front moved over the region with heavy snow and blowing snow late on the 14th through the morning of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP Observer in Pelican measured 13.4 inches of new snow at 8am on the 15th and was still snowing moderately. Their report the following day had another 2.1 inches of snow but had changed to rain. Another report from Facebook included a photo of around 1 foot of snow at the Inian Islands in the same zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front wrapping around a deep low in the Bering Sea moves northward bringing blizzard conditions to the North Slope.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Point Lay reported several hours of blizzard conditions. Blizzard conditions developed early in the morning hours and continued into the evening. The blizzard conditions at Point Lay were accompanied by wind gusts as high as 51 mph. Elsewhere, Wainwright reported strong winds all day with as high as 54 mph. The visibility in Wainwright was under a one mile for most of the day due to blowing snow, with the visibility occasionally dropping to as low as one quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front wrapping around a deep low in the Bering Sea moves northward bringing blizzard conditions to the North Slope.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed in Utqiagvik during the early morning hours of the 24th and continued through the late morning. The blizzard conditions were accompanied by wind gusts as high as 55 mph in Utqiagvik.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lingered over the NE gulf with an associated front causing very light, dry snow to fall for over 12 hours. A wave formed along the front causing rates to pick up to warning level rates for another 12 or more hours, particularly on the northeast side of the low as it started tracking SE along the outer coast late on the 24th and into the 25th. The dry consistency of the snow lead to blowing snow and drifting as the front moved across the southern panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer in Pelican measured 1.9 inches by 8am on the 24th, then another 9.6 inches the next morning for a storm total of 11.5 in approximately 26hrs. Another spotter in Pelican measured 6 inches of snow by 530pm on the 24th and 8 more inches by the following morning, for a storm total of 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure lingered over the NE gulf with an associated front causing very light, dry snow to fall for over 12 hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hoonah COOP measured a storm total of 9.1 inches with most of that falling in the latter 12 hours of the long duration event. Snow was dry and blowing around, which may have reduced the overall total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As one system moved out, another was quick to move into the region with a gale force front. The Haines Chilkat Peninsula saw a period of heavy snow the evening of the 10th followed by a change to rain the next morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very wet snow was reported by a trained spotter 7 miles south of Haines beginning around 6pm on the 10th. Snow became heavy by 7pm, measuring a whopping 6 inches by 1030pm. The same spotter measured an additional 3 inches by morning, then changed to rain around 7am. The COOP observer in town had measured 6.1 inches of snow with rain falling by 8am.

Noorvik had wind gusts up to 53 MPH while Kotzebue had gusts as high as 54 mph. The storm left residents in Kotzebue without power which also caused the city's water plant to shut down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather systems brought strong winds with periods of blizzard conditions as well as snow and freezing rain March 4th-8th 2023. An area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea along with an associated warm front, combined with strong high pressure over Interior Alaska initially brought blizzard conditions to the west coast. As temperatures warmed, snow eventually transitioned over to freezing rain in some locations, with strong winds persisting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed along the southern coast of the Seward Peninsula on the on the morning of the 4th, first at Nome and then at Golovin. The visibility improved in Nome by the evening of the 4th; however, in Golovin periods of blizzard conditions continued into the afternoon of the 5th. Winds during the event peaked at 43 mph in Golovin and 41 mph in Nome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad and elongated upper low draped across southern Alaska tracked slowly north on December 31, 2022; an embedded shortwave, associated with a strong surface low and deep southerly moisture fetch, tracked northward towards Prince William Sound. Of note was the warm temperatures aloft. As it was a fast moving pattern, some of the impacts were limited; however, areas of mixed precipitation, and heavy and blowing snow did impact the Chugach Mountains, the Turnagain Arm and Thompson Pass on January 1, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low elevation mixed precipitation and areas of heavy snow across the mountains and mountain passes, impacted the Turnagain Arm and areas along the Chugach Mountain Range. Alyeska Resort staff and a Chugach Avalanche forecaster reported that the area south of Turnagain Pass at had received 4-6 inches of new snow before 8:35AM, with the rain-snow level as low as 800-1000ft. Additionally, areas extending from Turnagain Pass through Portage Turnoff up to Indian Valley saw moderate to heavy mixed precipitation, with 2-4 inches of slush along Indian Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of Arctic air over parts of Southwest Alaska brought very cold temperatures and even colder wind chills to parts of the region, namely over the Kuskokwim Delta.  Temperatures dipped to as cold as -30 Fahrenheit on the morning of 1/21/23 for more interior areas along the Kuskokwim River near Aniak, and down to near -20 Fahrenheit near the coast.  Winds around 10 to 15 mph closer to the coast helped produce the coldest wind chills observed during the morning of 1/21, or around -40 Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bethel, Napakiak and Kwethluk AWOS observations all hit -40 wind chills at various points in time on the morning of 1/21/23 with north winds around 10 to 15 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front lifted north across the Aleutians and into the Bering Sea between 1/21 and 1/23, producing numerous impacts ranging from strong winds up to 86 mph across the eastern Aleutians to snow and blowing snow farther north across the Pribilof Islands and Kuskokwim Delta.  This front was able to interact favorably with Arctic air entrenched farther north across much of western Alaska and the northern Bering, facilitating a tight temperature and pressure gradient that helped to drive the strong winds and snow/blowing snow observed with this system as it progressed north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nikolski National Ocean Service observation site began recording strong easterly wind gusts above 75 mph by around 6 AM AKST 1/21 as the front approached from the south, with a peak gust of 86 mph recorded later in the day at about 1 PM.  A gust to 77 mph was also recorded at another mesonet site (DPLA2) in Dutch Harbor at around 4 PM the same day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather systems brought strong winds with periods of blizzard conditions as well as snow and freezing rain March 4th-8th 2023. An area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea along with an associated warm front, combined with strong high pressure over Interior Alaska initially brought blizzard conditions to the west coast. As temperatures warmed, snow eventually transitioned over to freezing rain in some locations, with strong winds persisting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed along the Chukchi Sea coast the morning of the 4th. Blizzard conditions continued to be reported by the Point Hope AWOS through the afternoon of the 5th. The Point Hope AWOS stopped reporting the visibility during the afternoon of the 5th; however, webcam continued to show areas of blizzard conditions persisting through the evening of the 6th. Winds gusted as high as 75 mph at Red Dog Dock and 61 mph at Point Hope during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to portions of western Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed on the Baldwin Peninsula and Selawik Valley the morning of the 25th and continued into the in early morning hours of the 26th. Winds at Kotzebue gusted as high as 67 mph. Noorvik had wind gusts to 58 mph and Selawik had gusts to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to portions of western Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds picked up the morning of the 25th as the front approached from the southwest. Blizzard conditions developed as the snow moved into the area. Visibility improved during the early morning hours of the 26th as winds diminished. Noatak had wind gusts as high as 54 mph during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to portions of western Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed late in the evening of the 24th and continued into the evening of the 25th along the western Arctic Coast.  Cape Lisburne had wind gusts as high as 100 mph while Wainwright had wind gusts as high as 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front lifted north across the Aleutians and into the Bering Sea between 1/21 and 1/23, producing numerous impacts ranging from strong winds up to 86 mph across the eastern Aleutians to snow and blowing snow farther north across the Pribilof Islands and Kuskokwim Delta.  This front was able to interact favorably with Arctic air entrenched farther north across much of western Alaska and the northern Bering, facilitating a tight temperature and pressure gradient that helped to drive the strong winds and snow/blowing snow observed with this system as it progressed north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong offshore winds combined with plenty of transportable snow lead to a long duration ground blizzard event across much of the Kuskokwim Delta from 1/22 to 1/23. The worst conditions were observed at the Kipnuk AWOS, where 1/4 mile or less visibility with blowing snow and sustained winds between 40 and 50 mph recorded for much of the day starting at 2 AM, lasting until at least 8:30 AM 1/23 before the sensor stopped reporting data.  Significant blowing snow with visibility reductions to 1/2 mile at times was also recorded across much of the rest of the Kuskokwim Delta during this time frame, including in and around Bethel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic setup for freezing rain across portion of Southwest Alaska took shape beginning on the afternoon of 1/27 as a warm front working north over the eastern Bering Sea and up the Alaska Peninsula slowly progressed north into a retreating Arctic air mass initially in place across most land areas of Southwest.  Temperatures at the surface were mainly at or below 15 Fahrenheit to start off the early morning across much of Bristol Bay and interior parts of the Kuskokwim River Valley.  Farther south, the warm front began to work north up along the Alaska Peninsula, pushing in southeast winds and temperatures in the mid 30s to low 40s in its wake.||The shallow cold air mass in place farther north with north to northeast winds holding on for most of the day at the surface allowed for a significant frontal overrunning event to occur, with a warm nose of air above freezing streaming out ahead of the surface warm front.  An inversion only a few 100 to few 1000 feet thick below the warm layer allowed for several hours of melting snow to fall as freezing rain at ground level in a number of favored locations across Bristol Bay.  About 0.25 inches of ice accretion was recorded at King Salmon during the morning hours of 1/27 before the front pushed through in the early afternoon and allowed temperatures at ground level to warm above freezing.||Farther north, northerly winds continued to dam cold air into the Kuskokwim Mountains for much longer, leading to many hours of accumulating freezing rain for Dillingham and Clark's Point, where nearly 0.5 inches of icing was recorded before the front finally pushed through and warmed surface temperatures above freezing during the evening hours of 1/27.||As the front continued to progress north into the Kuskokwim Delta, it began to accelerate, resulting in a faster transition from snow, to freezing rain, then to rain for areas between Quinhagak and Bethel as winds turned from northeast to southerly and as warm air began to flood in at the surface.  This resulted in much lighter ice accretion less than 0.1 inches and lower impacts for areas in and around Bethel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first report of significant ice accretion came in from a meteorological technician at the King Salmon Weather Service Office at 3 PM 1/27.  The technician reported it was like a skating rink, with 1/4 inch layered crust built up on the snow pack before temperatures warmed above freezing during the afternoon hours.  Meanwhile, the Dillingham AWOS reported continuous freezing rain beginning 11 AM 1/27 and lasting through about 10 PM.  By around this time, much of the area surrounding Dillingham began to see southeast winds warm temps above freezing and change remaining precip to rain, while Togiak also changed over to rain and hovered just above freezing starting earlier in the afternoon.||Numerous webcams around Dillingham also showed appreciable ice accretion on top of camera covers facing into the prevailing wind, resulting in distorted and blurry imagery from webcams that worsened as more ice continued to build up.||NWS Anchorage called the Lake and Peninsula Bourough Emergency Manager who reported there were some cars off the road in Naknek on 1/27 but no ice/freezing rain totals were reported.  In a follow-up conversation with the Dillingham fire chief, the worst impacts were reported, with just less than 1/2 inch of icing and most severe conditions on 1/27 reported. Precipitation changed to rain sometime late in the evening of 1/27 to early morning of 1/28. Road crews sanded in advance on 1/27, but roads got messy by the evening hours. Not a lot of people were traveling before 9AM on 1/28, with pretty bad conditions and some cars sliding off roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard Conditions developed in the late evening of the 3rd along the coast of the Yukon Delta, with poor visibility persisting through the morning of the 4th. Winds at Cape Romanzof peaked at 77 mph. The winds gusted as high as 47 mph at Scammon Bay, 46 mph at Chevak, 45 mph at Marshall, and 41 mph at St Mary's.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather systems brought strong winds with periods of blizzard conditions as well as snow and freezing rain March 4th-8th 2023. An area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea along with an associated warm front, combined with strong high pressure over Interior Alaska initially brought blizzard conditions to the west coast. As temperatures warmed, snow eventually transitioned over to freezing rain in some locations, with strong winds persisting.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed on on the Baldwin Peninsula on the morning of the 4th and continued through the morning of the 6th. Winds gusted as high as 52 mph during the event. The strong winds caused a citywide power outage in Kotzebue, which subsequently  caused the city's water system to fail. Significant drifting caused made travel in Kotzebue difficult for several days after the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad and elongated upper low draped across southern Alaska tracked slowly north on December 31, 2022; an embedded shortwave, associated with a strong surface low and deep southerly moisture fetch, tracked northward towards Prince William Sound. Of note was the warm temperatures aloft. As it was a fast moving pattern, some of the impacts were limited; however, areas of mixed precipitation, and heavy and blowing snow did impact the Chugach Mountains, the Turnagain Arm and Thompson Pass on January 1, 2023.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the front passed through Prince William Sound, it spread a heavy mix of rain and snow along and ahead of the front. The rain/snow mix mostly affected the coastline, while heavy and blowing snow created blizzard conditions along the mountains and mountain passes. DOT reported hazardous road conditions with heavy snow, whiteout conditions due to both heavy snow and blowing snow, and heavy drifting through Thompson Pass as snow amounts of 8 to 16 inches were noted and winds maxed out at 55 mph. Gareth with the Valdez Avalanche Center reported 20 from the summit of the pass north towards the Copper River Basin. Towards Valdez, heavy snow transitioned to a heavy mix from mile 20 through Keystone Canyon, which created very difficult to hazardous driving conditions. Snow transitioned to heavy rain from the Canyon towards Valdez.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Taku mountain wave winds developed over downtown Juneau as a NE pressure gradient tightened along the Coast Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Taku wind event resulted in numerous wind gusts in excess of 60 mph over nearly two days. These gusts were recorded by many stations across the downtown Juneau docks. One station, AJXA2, recorded a peak wind gust of 76 mph at 702 pm AKST March 11.

CZ_NAME: WESTERN KUPREANOF ISLAND AND KUIU ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure over the Gulf resulted in waves of precipitation moving into Southeast Alaska, initiating an overrunning snow event. The greatest snowfall totals were largely in parts of the northern panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Security Bay reported 8.5 inches of snow. The spotter later reported that the snow had changed to rain by 4 PM AKDT. Another spotter in Port Alexander estimated 8 inches of snow had fallen by 830am on the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure over the Gulf resulted in waves of precipitation moving into Southeast Alaska, initiating an overrunning snow event. The greatest snowfall totals were largely in parts of the northern panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Customs COOP measured 12.5 inches of snow by 7am on the 16th with heavy snow still falling. The COOP in town had 15.5 inches reported that morning but had changed to rain. Other reports from the area included a CoCoRaHs report of 18.3 inches by 6am. Haines Borough Schools were closed by 615am on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure over the Gulf resulted in waves of precipitation moving into Southeast Alaska, initiating an overrunning snow event. The greatest snowfall totals were largely in parts of the northern panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skagway COOP measured 10 inches of snow by 7am on the 16th with a change to rain just occurring near sea level. The Klondike Highway DOT report noted 12 inches of snow by their morning report, but was still snowing. The highway was closed later that day due additional snowfall and for avalanche danger. Extreme overnight snow led to the decision to close Skagway School for the day, March 16th 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of weak lift aloft and temperatures near freezing below the snow growth layer of the the atmosphere was able to facilitate a period of light freezing rain and drizzle across parts of the Kenai Peninsula, Matanuska Valley and Anchorage area during the afternoon of 1/29.  While ice accumulation was very light, it did not take much to cause significant travel disruptions, with multiple ditched cars and accidents reported along the Glenn Highway between Palmer and Anchorage on the afternoon of 1/29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS in Wasilla reported unknown precipitation type at around 4 PM AKST, verified to be mainly freezing rain based on other reports and nearby webcam imagery.  The Glenn Highway was reportedly very slick south of the Parks Highway and Glenn Highway interchange near Palmer during the same afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a 964 mb low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to the portions of the west coast of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds began to increase late in the evening of the 19th as the front approached from the south. Blizzard conditions develop in Nome the morning of the 20th as snow moved into the area. The visibility improved as the winds died down late in the afternoon. The winds in both Nome and Golovin gusted as high as 54 mph during the event, while winds gusted as high as 52 mph in White Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front lifted north across the Aleutians and into the Bering Sea between 1/21 and 1/23, producing numerous impacts ranging from strong winds up to 86 mph across the eastern Aleutians to snow and blowing snow farther north across the Pribilof Islands and Kuskokwim Delta.  This front was able to interact favorably with Arctic air entrenched farther north across much of western Alaska and the northern Bering, facilitating a tight temperature and pressure gradient that helped to drive the strong winds and snow/blowing snow observed with this system as it progressed north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saint George AWOS began to report 1/2 mile snow and blowing snow at 12:30 AKST 1/23 continuing through about 4 PM with sustained northeast winds between 35 and 40 mph.  Conditions persisted for many hours, eventually improving above 1 mile visibility late on the evening of 1/23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic setup for freezing rain across portion of Southwest Alaska took shape beginning on the afternoon of 1/27 as a warm front working north over the eastern Bering Sea and up the Alaska Peninsula slowly progressed north into a retreating Arctic air mass initially in place across most land areas of Southwest.  Temperatures at the surface were mainly at or below 15 Fahrenheit to start off the early morning across much of Bristol Bay and interior parts of the Kuskokwim River Valley.  Farther south, the warm front began to work north up along the Alaska Peninsula, pushing in southeast winds and temperatures in the mid 30s to low 40s in its wake.||The shallow cold air mass in place farther north with north to northeast winds holding on for most of the day at the surface allowed for a significant frontal overrunning event to occur, with a warm nose of air above freezing streaming out ahead of the surface warm front.  An inversion only a few 100 to few 1000 feet thick below the warm layer allowed for several hours of melting snow to fall as freezing rain at ground level in a number of favored locations across Bristol Bay.  About 0.25 inches of ice accretion was recorded at King Salmon during the morning hours of 1/27 before the front pushed through in the early afternoon and allowed temperatures at ground level to warm above freezing.||Farther north, northerly winds continued to dam cold air into the Kuskokwim Mountains for much longer, leading to many hours of accumulating freezing rain for Dillingham and Clark's Point, where nearly 0.5 inches of icing was recorded before the front finally pushed through and warmed surface temperatures above freezing during the evening hours of 1/27.||As the front continued to progress north into the Kuskokwim Delta, it began to accelerate, resulting in a faster transition from snow, to freezing rain, then to rain for areas between Quinhagak and Bethel as winds turned from northeast to southerly and as warm air began to flood in at the surface.  This resulted in much lighter ice accretion less than 0.1 inches and lower impacts for areas in and around Bethel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around Bethel began seeing snow mix with freezing around 5-6 PM 1/27 with clear evidence if ice buildup on the Napakiak webcam cover facing into the wind, and similarly at Bethel slightly later in the evening. Freezing rain was also reported at the Bethel ASOS at 9 PM 1/27.  Temperatures warmed above freezing near 12 AM 1/28 and changed the freezing rain and snow mix to all rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 990 mb low moved north through the Bering Strait, bringing blizzard conditions to Kotzebue.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed in Kotzebue late in the morning of the 1st as the surface low moved through the Bering Strait. Winds died down in the evening as the low moved away resulting in improved visibility. Winds in Kotzebue gusted as high as 44 mph during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moved through the region with a surge of strong southerly winds late on the 8th and into the early morning hours of Easter Sunday causing rain at sea level and high elevation snow for White Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT Klondike Highway report noted 3 to 6 inches of snow between milepost 12 to the border with a mix of rain and snow at the time of the report. Based on timing of when rain began and ended from the Skagway airport (sea level) and temperature changes at Fraser Camp, north of the border, it is estimated that snow fell over a period of less than 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving around a broader low in the Gulf of Alaska saw an embedded band of convective activity develop with strong cold air advection behind it, which resulted in an area of enhanced winds which tracked northward across the Inside Passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports were received that a storm blew trees onto Mitkof Highway and snapped two power poles near the Crystal Lake Hydroelectric Plant. The bulk of the damage occurred between 3 and 18 mile Mitkof Highway. around approximately 12:30 PM AKDT (11:30 AM AKST). Customers in that area were without power on Easter Sunday for more than 5 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cool air mass settling into the Copper Basin near a deep upper level trough centered over Mainland Alaska was able to interact favorably with a series of weak upper shortwaves that moved into the Copper Basin from the Yukon to produce widespread snowfall.  This was able to occur near the diurnal minimum surface temperatures for several hours during the late evening of 5/30 to the early morning of 5/31.  Most of the higher amounts fell at higher elevations near the Alaska Range, including up to 10 inches near Paxson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of accumulating snowfall were relayed by the Alaska Department of Transportation, most of which fell from about 11 PM 5/30 to the early morning hours of 5/31.  Around 6 inches fell over Glennallen, with mostly 1-2 inches of slush reported farther south and west.  Heavier snowfall was reported along the Tok Cutoff and northern Richardson Highway, including up to 10 inches in Paxson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure and frontal system moved up from the North Pacific and along the Aleutians while it's associated front moved up the Alaska Peninsula, then past Kodiak Island and into the southern Mainland.  Various impacts were felt across large portions of the southern Alaska and Bering areas, ranging from blizzard conditions across the Pribilof Islands and Kuskokwim Delta to heavy snow and blowing snow across Bristol Bay, Kodiak Island and much of the Kenai Peninsula.  Well entrenched Arctic air sitting in place across most of Mainland Alaska out ahead of this system helped to support a large baroclinic zone along and ahead of this system's frontal feature, supporting the moderate snowfall totals observed across Bristol Bay, Kodiak Island and portions of the Kenai Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saint George AWOS began reporting blizzard conditions around 5 PM AKST 2/9 and lasted at least intermittently all the way until about 9 AM AKST 2/10.  Snow and blowing snow with 1/4 mile visibility and north to northeast wind sustained at 40 to 55 mph with gust as high as 70 mph occurred during this time period.  Conditions at Saint Paul remained much better, where there was little falling/blowing snow and slightly weaker winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 960 mb low moved north through the Bering Sea, bringing blizzard conditions to the northern portion of the Seward Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the low approached the area, wind speeds picked up  and snow developed along the north coast of the Seward Peninsula. Buckland saw blizzard conditions for about a four hours on the evening of the 1st, with winds topping out at 46 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving from west to east along the Arctic Coast brought blizzard conditions to the Beaufort Sea coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at Barter Island on the evening of the 8th and continued through the morning of the 9th. Winds gusted as high as 54 mph at Barter Island during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a 964 mb low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to the portions of the west coast of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds began to increase the evening of the 19th as the front approached the St. Lawrence Island and the Bering Strait Coast. Blizzard conditions developed as snow associated with the front began falling just after midnight on the 20th, with blizzard conditions continuing through the early morning hours of the 21st. During the event, winds gusted as high as 67 mph at Gambell, 67 mph at Tin City, 60 mph at Teller, 59 mph at Savoonga and 45 mph at Bervig Mission.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a 964 mb low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to the portions of the west coast of Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds increased the evening of the 19th as the front approached from the south. Blizzard conditions developed in the early morning hours of the 20th. The visibility improved the evening of the 20th as winds decreased. Cape Romanzof reported wind gusts as high as 87 mph during the event. Elsewhere, winds gusted as high as 62 mph in Chevak, 58 mph in Scammon Bay, 51 mph in Marshall and 54 mph in St. Mary's.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to portions of western Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed late in the morning of the 25th when a front moved over the area, with blizzard conditions persisting into the early morning hours of the 26th. Point Hope had gusts to 61 mph, while Red Dog Dock had gusts to 84 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to portions of western Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed in Nome for several hours in the late morning hours of the 25th. In the early afternoon, conditions improved briefly; however, blizzard conditions returned in late afternoon and continued through the evening hours. Nome had gusts as high as 41 mph during the event while Golovin had gusts as high as 53 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to portions of western Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed along the Bering Strait Coast the morning of the 25th and continued through at least the evening of the 25th. Winds were gusting to 71 mph at Teller when the AWOS stopped reporting data for the event. Elsewhere during the event, Tin City had gusts to 64 mph and Brevig Mission had gusts to 61 mph.

CZ_NAME: S. SLOPES OF ERN BROOKS RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front tracked eastward across Interior Alaska, bringing snow, heavy at times to portions of the western and central Interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer in Wiseman reported 10 inches of snow in a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front tracked eastward across Interior Alaska, bringing snow, heavy at times to portions of the western and central Interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alaska Department of Transportaion reported 10 inches of snow fell in a 24-hour period at Gobblers Knob.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observer at Manley Hot Springs reported 7.5 inches of snow in a 24 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a low moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to portions of western Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Ambler city office reported a foot of snow fell in a 24 hour period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Retired NWS employee reported a period of heavy snow between noon and 3pm on March 15th where 4 to 5 inches fell. Additional periods of heavy snow fell through the night on the 15th with winds gusting to around 25 mph. It is unknown what the storm total was.

Various impacts were felt across large portions of the southern Alaska and Bering areas, ranging from blizzard conditions across the Pribilof Islands and Kuskokwim Delta to heavy snow and blowing snow across Bristol Bay, Kodiak Island and much of the Kenai Peninsula.  Well entrenched Arctic air sitting in place across most of Mainland Alaska out ahead of this system helped to support a large baroclinic zone along and ahead of this system's frontal feature, supporting the moderate snowfall totals observed across Bristol Bay, Kodiak Island and portions of the Kenai Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Webcam images from Whittier tunnel staging area showed widespread low visibilities with snow and blowing snow during daylight hours on 2/10.  The Portage AWOS reported 1/4 mile visibility snow and blowing snow between about 10 AM and 6 PM AKST 2/10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying North Pacific low entered the Bering Sea during the evening of February 27, tracking northeast toward the Bering Strait. The low brought high winds to the Pribilof Islands as it passed to the north of the islands at peak intensity during the early morning hours of February 28. At the same time, its front pushed into Southwest Alaska, leading to widespread blowing snow, with blizzard conditions along the Kuskokwim Delta coast.||The front further progressed eastward into Southcentral Alaska, where it dropped heavy snow and blowing snow lead to low visibilities. This was aided by the development of a triple point low along the front in the Gulf of Alaska late on February 28 into the morning hours of March 1. Snow came to an end for most locations on March 1 as the front and newly-developed triple point low continued eastward. However, impacts continued on Kodiak Island as cold air advection on the back side of the exiting low contributed to high winds through the morning of March 2.||See the previous month for information on the February 2023 portion of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were observed by the Whittier MAWS from the morning of March 1 through early afternoon. Visibility was 1/4 miles or less, with winds gusting in excess of 35 knots (40 mph). Visibility improved by the afternoon and evening as the intensity of snowfall decreased.

Blizzard conditions were also observed along the Kuskokwim Delta coast, at the Kipnuk AWOS, for several hours. Wind gusts at Kipnuk consistently exceeded 40 knots (46 mph).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet observation in Saint Paul recorded a wind gust to 70 knots (81 mph) around 2 AM on February 28 as a low pressure system in the Bering Sea passed north of the island near peak intensity.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Platinum AWOS recorded blizzard conditions from the evening of February 27 through the early morning hours of February 28. Winds were in excess of 30 knots (35 mph), with consistent gusts to 45 knots (52 mph). While wind observations dropped out after only 2 hours of observed blizzard-like conditions, they returned (still meeting blizzard criteria) around 1 AM AKST, just before visibility improved. This, in combination with nearby wind observations, suggests that the blizzard was sustained until the front pushed ashore and snow ended early on February 28. The Manokotak AWOS also recorded several hours of blowing snow conditions with 1/2 mile visibility or lower. Many other observing sites across the Bristol Bay region recorded at least a brief period of 1/2 mile visibility due to snow/blowing snow as the front pushed eastward through the morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure developed over the northern Gulf of Alaska bringing significant moisture to Southeast Alaska. Cold air entrenched over the region at low levels ahead of the event allowed for significant snow to fall across the area in two major pulses. The first being along the warm front, with a period of blizzard conditions over Juneau, and the second pulse occurred as the cold front moved across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late on February 28th and became heavy through the following morning with periodic gusts of around 25mph. A change to rain happened in the afternoon. The COOP station at the Petersburg airport measured 6 inches by 1248pm with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour and significant settling. A storm total of 9 inches was measured at their official measurement time of 9pm that night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moving around a broader low in the Gulf of Alaska saw an embedded band of convective activity develop with strong cold air advection behind it, which resulted in an area of enhanced winds which tracked northward across the Inside Passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to several mesonet stations around downtown Juneau reporting wind gusts of around 60 mph, the Juneau Airport ASOS measured a peak gust of 71 mph at 3:46 PM AKDT. AEL&P utility company reported a few small power outages around Juneau due to the winds and tree branches coming down. A weather service employee noted multiple trees down on their property on North Douglas as did the Auke Bay Elementary School.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began late on February 28th and became heavy through the following morning with periodic gusts of around 30mph. A change to rain happened in the late morning. A trained spotter south of Wrangell measured 6 inches of dense snow.

A change to rain happened in the late morning. A trained spotter in Craig measured 5 inches of snow, blizzard like conditions and a power outage at 545am. A CoCoRaHS observer in Edna Bay measured 12 inches of snow by 7am. Thorne Bay COOP on the east side of the island measured 4 inches from this event.

The COOP on the north side of town measured 8 inches from this event and noted a change to rain at 10am. At 703am the City of Ketchikan delayed opening for 2 hours.

An NWS Employee measured 4.3 inches of snow and a power outage at 7am. The COOP in Metlakatla measured 2.1 inches of snow at 1148am, but noted that there was a large amount of snow overnight that had transitioned into rain with high winds. The first being along the warm front, with a period of blizzard conditions over Juneau, and the second pulse occurred as the cold front moved across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in Yakutat in the afternoon of February 28th and continued to fall for the next two days. Around 8am on the 1st, WSO Yakutat estimated 6 to 8 inches of snow had fallen while a retired NWS employee nearby measured 9 inches and was a quarter mile visibility at the time. By 11am another 8 inches had fallen. The quarter mile visibility lasted from 544am to 136pm on the 1st, with winds of 15 to 20 mph. The storm total by 446pm had reached 22 inches, but post frontal snow showers continued through the following morning with around 7 inches of additional accumulation.

By late afternoon reports were up to 2 feet of snow in town. Strong wind and snow continued through the morning of the 2nd with gusts of 30 to 40mph and storm totals of 26 to 35 inches in town and 15.8 inches measured at the border.
