CZ_NAME: NORTH SLOPES OF THE WESTERN ALASKA RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and fast moving low moved from Bristol Bay north to Kotzebue Sound on November 28th. This caused very strong winds to develop in the Western Alaska Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts in excess of 70 mph were reported at Farewell (L20KX) USARRAY site, with a peak wind gust of 85 mph measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 13, 2023, front associated with a 988 mb low in the Bering Sea brought high winds to the Bering Strait Coast, St. Lawrence Island, as well as the Yukon Delta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed along the Bering Strait Coast as well as St. Lawrence Island on the evening of May 13th and continued into the early morning hours of May 14th. Teller reported wind gusts as high as 76 mph during this event. Wales reported gusts as high as 62 mph before the wind sensor failed during the middle of the event. Elsewhere, Tin City reported wind gusts as high as 60 mph, while Brevig Mission reported gusts as high as 59 mph and Gambell reported gusts as high as 52 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 13, 2023, front associated with a 988 mb low in the Bering Sea brought high winds to the Bering Strait Coast, St. Lawrence Island, as well as the Yukon Delta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High Winds developed over the Yukon Delta the afternoon of the 13th and continued into the early morning hours on the 14th. Cape Romanzof reported gusts as high as 61 mph during the event. Elsewhere, Mountain Village reported gusts as high as 54 mph, while Scammon Bay, Marshall and St. Mary's all reported wind gusts as high as 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the Kuskokwim Valley paired with a trough of low pressure extending across the Gulf of Alaska which brought heavy snow to south central and west central Alaska from the Kuskokwim Basin south and east across the Susitna, Matanuska and Anchorage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6.0 inches was measured NW of Palmer on Fishhook Road and additional reports of snowfall of 6+ inches was reported from Sutton to Palmer to Wasilla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the Kuskokwim Valley paired with a trough of low pressure extending across the Gulf of Alaska which brought heavy snow to south central and west central Alaska from the Kuskokwim Basin south and east across the Susitna, Matanuska and Anchorage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall at Big Lake was measured at 9.0 inches. A later observation would bring the storm total to 11.5 inches by 10:57am on 11/13/23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the Kuskokwim Valley paired with a trough of low pressure extending across the Gulf of Alaska which brought heavy snow to south central and west central Alaska from the Kuskokwim Basin south and east across the Susitna, Matanuska and Anchorage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reported 13.1 inches of snowfall in the Bayshore/Klatt area of Anchorage.  Chugach Electric reported 2600 customers without power and commercial power was out at the NWS Office for much of the day.  Increased snow load led to further tree damage with drooping tree branches and fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving in the Gulf of Alaska tapped into deep subtropical moisture to produce heavy precipitation along the coast in Western Prince William Sound. Strong forcing and low-level instability combined with ample moisture to push precipitation inland toward Turnagain Pass. Snowfall rates exceeded one inch per hour as the low's front pushed inland on November 29, leading to new snowfall accumulations in excess of one foot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summit Lake SNOTEL measured 14 new inches of snow on November 29. The heaviest snow occurred from late morning through the evening hours, with snowfall rates in excess of one inch per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low over the Kuskokwim Valley paired with a trough of low pressure extending across the Gulf of Alaska which brought heavy snow to south central and west central Alaska from the Kuskokwim Basin south and east across the Susitna, Matanuska and Anchorage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sleetmute, AK Post Office reported 4-6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving up into the Gulf of Alaska was able to interact favorably with Arctic air and strong surface high pressure entrenched over Mainland Alaska to create a very strong northeast to southwest pressure gradient and very strong wind that channeled through gaps in terrain.  The strongest winds were focused near and to the southwest of the pass between the Copper River Basin and Matanuska Valley, as well as through Thompson Pass and into Valdez.  Recent rounds of snowfall were enough for these winds to resuspend fallen snow and create ground blizzard conditions in both locations.  The winds gusting between 60 and 70 mph on 11/20 in the Matanuska Valley were also enough to knock out power to thousands of customers at the height of the wind storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alaska Department of Transportation reported very strong winds gusting to 80 mph through Thompson Pass at 8:30 AM AKST 11/20 with blowing snow so severe they could not see the front of their plows.  These winds were also verified by a mesonet station in Thompson Pass that recorded a wind gust of 89 mph at 9:10 AM AKST 11/20.  Blizzard conditions continued through the evening with winds gusting between 50 and 70 mph through the pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening low moving rapidly northward over the panhandle brought high winds to the southern panhandle and to Juneau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Employee reported a rooftop wind gust at the WSO of 64mph at approximately 0545AKST, along with the power being out on Annette Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening low moving rapidly northward over the panhandle brought high winds to the southern panhandle and to Juneau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 75mph was measured at 0622AKST at the Hydaburg Seaplane Base. Other areas on Prince of Wales Island also measured sustained winds above 40mph for a period of 1 to 3 hours and the Thorne Bay RAWS had a peak gust of 67mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of well-below-average spring temperatures combined with an above-average winter snowpack created a dynamic breakup of the Mosquito Fork of the Fortymile River. This resulted in ice jams and flooding. April temperatures were well below normal, and when temperatures began to warm in early May, it melted the low-elevation snowpack quickly. Because of the relatively quick addition of snowmelt onto strong ice, the ice was more susceptible to jamming and floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam formed on the Mosquito Fork of the Fortymile River approximately 1.5 miles downstream from the Taylor Highway bridge. A NWS automated river gage on the bridge recorded a rise in water levels of 10 feet in 48 hours. Ice rose to the base of the bridge and lower part of the guardrail but damage to the bridge was not observed. Water also went overbank and flooded portions of a wayside at the bridge but the Taylor Highway was not impacted by floodwaters and the road remained open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of well-below-average spring temperatures combined with an above-average winter snowpack created a dynamic breakup of the Yukon River. This resulted in numerous ice jams and snowmelt flooding on the Yukon River. Breakup flooding began on May 12th in eastern Alaska in Eagle and ended on June 3rd in the Yukon River delta at the Bering Sea coast at the community of Emmonak. The primary cause of this active flood year was a 1-2 week delay in breakup when compared to typical timing. April temperatures were well below normal, and when temperatures began to warm in early May, it melted the low-elevation snowpack quickly. Because of the relatively quick addition of snowmelt onto strong ice, the ice was more susceptible to jamming and floods. In addition to the late breakup, the snowpack this winter was 1 to 1.5 times greater than the climatological normal for interior and western Alaska. Significant ice jams and associated flooding on the Yukon River in Canada resulted in large volumes of ice and water to move down the Yukon River into Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record flooding occurred on the evening of May 13th at Circle. The Riverwatch team flew between Eagle and Circle on the afternoon of May 13th. During the day on May 13th, an observer 28 miles upriver of Circle reported the highest water he���d ever seen. The Riverwatch team observed widespread flooding (trees inundated, islands covered in ice, river well out of bank) upriver from Circle. Ice had degraded during the day in warm conditions and the Riverwatch team saw ice start to move while they were in Circle at 5 pm May 13th. Prior to landing, the Riverwatch team observed a roughly 90-mile-long run of ice behind the breakup front. For context, that is the longest run of ice that this Riverwatch team could remember ever seeing. The Riverwatch team circled above several times after takeoff and observed ice moving, and progressive disintegration of larger ice sheets (indicating weakness and that given the momentum of the river ice jams were unlikely). The flooding came on quickly; the water rose extremely fast and major flooding was first reported at around 8 pm. There was extensive damage, with flood waters estimated to be higher than the previous flood of record, 1945. The store had water up to the ceiling of the first floor. Ice moved into town and damaged homes with stranded ice throughout the community. Most structures in the town flooded and also flooded the airport apron and taxiway. The Riverwatch team flew over the next day and did not observe evidence of ice jamming but believe that the river simply slowed at the S-curves (where the Yukon becomes more braided) and compounded with historically high water levels. By the next morning, floodwaters had receded and had dropped below the airport access road by 3 pm May 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of well-below-average spring temperatures combined with an above-average winter snowpack created a dynamic breakup of the Buckland River. This resulted in numerous ice jams. April temperatures were well below normal, and when temperatures began to warm in early May, it melted the low-elevation snowpack quickly. Because of the relatively quick addition of snowmelt onto strong ice, the ice was more susceptible to jamming and floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred in Buckland due to several ice jams on the Buckland River from May 17th to 19th. An ice jam located approximately 1 mile upriver from the community resulted in significant out-of-bank flow that was eventually routed around the jam and into the community of Buckland. Several additional jams were located on nearly all subsequent bends of the Buckland River downstream of Buckland. Backwater from these jams was also adding to the inundation of the community and surrounding tundra. Approximately 80% of the community was inundated with water, with water under or surrounding most structures; most roads were inundated and residents had to travel around town in boats; the road to the airport was only passable by boat. On May 19th, the Riverwatch team was informed by a trained spotter in Buckland that the upstream jam had been released, as did the downstream jams, and water was receding quickly and the community was no longer flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated conditions from preceding storms, combined with widespread heavy rainfall of 1-2 inches over a 24 hour period, across the Susitna and Matanuska River Valleys Sept 1, 2023.  These conditions and widespread, consistent rainfall pushed streams to bankfull with large swaths of the valleys experiencing minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains of 1-2 inches in 24 hours caused widespread minor flooding across the Talkeetna and Little Susitna Basins.  Minor flooding was recorded at several river gauges and long periods of elevated river levels, exacerbated river bank erosion in the areas around Talkeetna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated conditions from preceding storms, combined with widespread heavy rainfall of 1-2 inches over a 24 hour period, across the Susitna and Matanuska River Valleys Sept 1, 2023.  These conditions and widespread, consistent rainfall pushed streams to bankfull with large swaths of the valleys experiencing minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains in the Matanuska Valley, specifically around Hatcher's Pass and the Enchanted Forest Subdivision in Houston, AK, reported minor flooding from 1-2 inches of rainfall that fell over the course of the 24 hour period September 1, 2023.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Snow Glacial Dam and Skilak Glacial Dam both released in early September causing highwater and widespread minor to moderate severity flooding from above Skilak Lake, AK downstream to Soldotna, AK.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations of Snow Glacier indicated that a Glacial Dammed Lake (GDL) released on or around the 9/7/23 to 9/8/23.  The Snow and Kenai Rivers rose rapidly in response, routing flood waters through the system from the GDL.  The Snow River reached action stage, however as the flood propagated downstream it combined with high river baseflows to crest in minor flood at 13.48 feet at 3:15pm AKDT on 9/10/23 at the Cooper Landing river gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Snow Glacial Dam and Skilak Glacial Dam both released in early September causing highwater and widespread minor to moderate severity flooding from above Skilak Lake, AK downstream to Soldotna, AK.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Skilak Glacier Dammed Lake released, combining with flow coming through Skilak Lake, impacting the region below Skilak Lake and Soldotna.  Widespread flooding was reported at Kenai Keys and along the Kenai River, especially below Skilak Lake where flood levels reached moderate severity. Verification was made using inundation maps matched with ground truth photos, emergency manager reports and social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over a short period of time, on already saturated soils, caused rapid rises and minor flooding along Chester Creek, in downtown Anchorage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Radar indicated that 0.5-1.0 inches of rainfall fell the evening of September 20, leading to a rapid response along Chester Creek at its intersection with Arctic Boulevard.  Soil conditions in the Chester Creek Basin were already saturated, causing the heavy rainfall to flood nearby bike paths and low lying areas along Chester Creek. Heavy rains also caused some storm drainages to back up and flood roads in low lying areas of Anchorage in the Chester Creek Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another major eruption, part of an eruptive sequence lasting several months at the Shishaldin Volcano, occurred, starting around 4 AM AKDT September 25th, continuing for several hours that morning. The ash column reached an estimated peak height of 45,000 feet; this plume was steered away from the volcano near False Pass towards the southeast by prevailing northwest flow at the time of the eruption.  This brought the ash plume across several towns along the southern end of the Alaska Peninsula, where numerous reports indicated minor ashfall and suspended ash over the course of the morning.  Reports included pilot and on the ground reports of ash and visible ash appearing on runways and other surfaces in places including False Pass, King Cove, Cold Bay and Sand Point.  The most notable ash accumulation measured at 1/16th inch in parts of False Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports were received of ashfall in the communities of False Pass, Cold Bay, Sand Point and King Cove.  The most noteworthy report was a measurement of 1/16th inch of ashfall in False Pass, the closest community to the volcano.  There were also reports of volcanic lightning and thunder from residents in False Pass.  Trace ashfall was reported by pilots and residents in several other locations around the southern Alaska Peninsula, in some cases reported as rain mixed with ash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force weather front along with a strong Atmospheric River (AR) associated with this system moved into the central Gulf of Alaska through the morning hours of September 20th. As the AR push east over southeast Alaska through the afternoon and evening hours of September 20th heavy rain moved over the central and north central inner channels. This heavy rain persisted into Thursday September 21st and a mesoscale frontal wave formed along the cold front which stalled the heavy rain and high rain rates over the central and north central inner channels through the evening and early morning hours of Friday September 22nd. The AR pushed east of the area by late Friday morning into the afternoon hours.  Strong wind gusts from 40 to 55mph were reported at sea level with higher winds gusts at high elevation weather stations as system moved through the area.||The region received from 3 to 6 inches with localized higher amounts near 9 inches at higher elevation sites. Most of the precipitation fell on September 21st. This persistent heavy rain produced minor flooding along small and flashy streams like Indian River near Sitka in the evening of September 20th, Montana Creek and Auke Lake near Juneau on September 21, and along Falls Creek near Petersburg on September 21 into the 22nd. Jordan Creek in Juneau did produce moderate flooding and impacted a few homes in the area through the evening of September 21 and then the morning hours of the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved into the Juneau area in the late afternoon of September 20th and continued to increase through the overnight hours with 1.5 to 2 inches of rainfall by 0700AKDT. Jordan Creek near the Juneau airport start to respond to this heavy rain with a sharp rise upward and went over minor flood stage of 9.7 feet in the early morning hours of September 21st. As the heavy rain continued, Jordan Creek rose to be just under moderate flood stage of 10.5 feet by the afternoon.   A second pulse of heavy rain moved over the area in the evening and overnight hours of the 21st and this additional 1 to 1.5 inches of rain pushed Jordan Creek above moderate flood stage. The creek crested at 10.77 feet in the early morning hours of Friday September 22nd. As the heavy rain diminished with the AR pushing east into Canada, Jordan Creek fell below moderate flood stage by mid morning and then below minor flood stage in the afternoon. ||There was moderate flooding around Jordan Creek which impacted roads, business and a few homes. The severity of the impacts was water getting into a few homes and crawlspaces, over driveways and around entrances to businesses. Meanwhile, shortly after 12AM on the 22nd, water coming down the hillside in downtown Juneau resulted in a few homes being evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of well-below-average spring temperatures combined with an above-average winter snowpack created a dynamic breakup of the Yukon River. This resulted in numerous ice jams and snowmelt flooding on the Yukon River. Breakup flooding began on May 12th in eastern Alaska in Eagle and ended on June 3rd in the Yukon River delta at the Bering Sea coast at the community of Emmonak. The primary cause of this active flood year was a 1-2 week delay in breakup when compared to typical timing. April temperatures were well below normal, and when temperatures began to warm in early May, it melted the low-elevation snowpack quickly. Because of the relatively quick addition of snowmelt onto strong ice, the ice was more susceptible to jamming and floods. In addition to the late breakup, the snowpack this winter was 1 to 1.5 times greater than the climatological normal for interior and western Alaska. Significant ice jams and associated flooding on the Yukon River in Canada resulted in large volumes of ice and water to move down the Yukon River into Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The breakup front progressed towards Fort Yukon on May 14th, finally stopping (jamming) just below Fort Yukon by late afternoon/evening. Water levels rose rapidly over bank and resulted in moderate flooding of the community. The river crested the night of the 14th. Several homes/structures were inundated with water. The tribal hall was flooded with water just up to the floor level with likely damage to the floor.  The road to the east of the Tribal Hall was breached to help reduce water levels with water flowing through the breach and back towards the Yukon River. The levee held well and prevented more significant damages. A local observer reported that water levels came to within one foot of overtopping the low spots along the levee, and estimated that the water level was similar to 1981. Water started receding early in the morning of May 15th when the ice jam released. The water took several days to fully drop back within bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of well-below-average spring temperatures combined with an above-average winter snowpack created a dynamic breakup of the Yukon River. This resulted in numerous ice jams and snowmelt flooding on the Yukon River. Breakup flooding began on May 12th in eastern Alaska in Eagle and ended on June 3rd in the Yukon River delta at the Bering Sea coast at the community of Emmonak. The primary cause of this active flood year was a 1-2 week delay in breakup when compared to typical timing. April temperatures were well below normal, and when temperatures began to warm in early May, it melted the low-elevation snowpack quickly. Because of the relatively quick addition of snowmelt onto strong ice, the ice was more susceptible to jamming and floods. In addition to the late breakup, the snowpack this winter was 1 to 1.5 times greater than the climatological normal for interior and western Alaska. Significant ice jams and associated flooding on the Upper Yukon River resulted in large volumes of ice and water to move down into the Lower Yukon River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the lower Yukon River at Russian Mission, an ice jam formed downriver from Russian Mission near Marshall on May 21st. Major flooding began May 22nd with complete submersion of the airport runway, inundating 2-3 homes, and other miscellaneous structures. Additional homes and structures were completely surrounded by flood water for four days. The ice jam released on May 24th, which resulted in a gradual drop in water levels as the large volume of impounded water from the extensive lowland flooding between Holy Cross and Russian Mission drained. An ice jam flood warning was issued on May 22nd and was canceled on May 27th. Flood advisory products were continued until June 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of well-below-average spring temperatures combined with an above-average winter snowpack created a dynamic breakup of the Yukon River. This resulted in numerous ice jams and snowmelt flooding on the Yukon River. Breakup flooding began on May 12th in eastern Alaska in Eagle and ended on June 3rd in the Yukon River delta at the Bering Sea coast at the community of Emmonak. The primary cause of this active flood year was a 1-2 week delay in breakup when compared to typical timing. April temperatures were well below normal, and when temperatures began to warm in early May, it melted the low-elevation snowpack quickly. Because of the relatively quick addition of snowmelt onto strong ice, the ice was more susceptible to jamming and floods. In addition to the late breakup, the snowpack this winter was 1 to 1.5 times greater than the climatological normal for interior and western Alaska. Significant ice jams and associated flooding on the Yukon River in Canada resulted in large volumes of ice and water to move down the Yukon River into Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jam flooding started late at night on May 12th and when the Riverwatch team landed in Eagle on May 13th (early afternoon) flood waters had receded leaving stranded ice well above the bank. The USGS river gage had a preliminary crest of 42 feet at 5 AM ADT on May 13th. Moderate flood impacts included damage to the seawall with the guardrail along the seawall was destroyed, stranded ice, and flooding on the road between Eagle and Eagle Village leaving the road impassable for a week due to stranded ice. Several structures in town were damaged by ice and floodwaters, including the Falcon Inn which had a high water mark of almost 2 feet into the finished basement. Preceding the ice jam, the river was at or above bank full, with Mission Road beginning to flood late afternoon of May 12th.||Prior to the Eagle event, there were multiple significant ice jams in Canada, including a 30-mile-long jam with the toe of the ice jam located approximately 30 miles upriver from Eagle on May 12th. This jam caused major flooding in Canada. Downstream of Eagle was a mix of intact ice sheets, open water, and an ice jam 8 miles downriver from the month of the Tatonduk River. Thus, conditions were ripe for ice jams to impact Eagle once the Canadian ice jam released, which occurred late at night on May 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 23rd, several thunderstorms moved through the Interior bringing small hail, frequent lighting and heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit a truck causing a barrel of gas in the back to ignite, blowing out the car's window. The driver of the truck sustained facial injures due to the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front brought high freezing levels and heavy rain to the Brooks Range. The combination of rainfall and snowmelt runoff from a late winter snowpack resulted in minor flooding along the Koyukuk River and tributaries draining the Brooks Range. Rainfall totals ranged from 1 to 2 inches between June 8th to the 10th, with the bulk of the rain falling in less than 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water levels on the Koyukuk River at Allakaket rose overbank in low-lying areas of the community near the river on June 10th. Minor flooding was reported when the water crested early Monday morning June 12th. Water inundated the old runway by the river and overtopped Stanley Slough Road near the boat launch. A local observer reported that the high water and debris was initially coming from the Alatna River on June 10th but then coming down the Koyukuk River by June 11th. It is estimated water levels would need to rise approximately 1 additional foot to go over roads in the lower part of the village and have more significant impacts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking toward south-central Alaska swept a front from south to north over the panhandle on the 23rd. This front brought heavy snow to the upper elevations of the Haines and Klondike Highways and high wind to the southern half of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high wind event from an advancing front resulted in an extended duration of high winds. The AWOS at the Hydaburg Seaplane base recorded a peak wind gust of 85 mph at 333 PM AKST December 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elongated low which was centered south of the eastern Aleutians moved into Southwest Alaska spreading heavy snow, strong winds and blizzard conditions across the region.  Blizzard conditions were observed at several sites from Dillingham north into interior Bristol Bay from late morning through the evening of Dec 10, where winds were strongest. At least 6 inches of snowfall was measured in King Salmon, with light snow continuing through Dec 11 with moisture wrapping around the back side of the low as it elongated along the Alaska/Aleutian Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FAA webcams and ASOS' recorded blizzard conditions as winds gusted as high as 40-45 kts (49 mph) through several hours at Clarks Point and Dillingham with visibilities dropping to 1/4 mile for several hours at each location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low tracked into Prince William Sound on Dec 12 as it rotated around a deep upper-level trough centered over Western Alaska. Snow spread west from the Copper Basin as the trough became northwest-southeast oriented. Out west, the upper-level trough contributed to a strong push of cold air across the Bering Sea, which lead to blowing snow with widespread snow showers across the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee measured 6 of snow located 3 miles east of office over a 9 hour period. Storm totals: 9.9 Strawberry and Northwood, 9-11 inches in southeast Anchorage. 11-14 inches on the Anchorage upper hillside. 8.8 inches in Eagle River. Information from LSRs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low tracked into Prince William Sound on Dec 12 as it rotated around a deep upper-level trough centered over Western Alaska. Snow spread west from the Copper Basin as the trough became northwest-southeast oriented. Out west, the upper-level trough contributed to a strong push of cold air across the Bering Sea, which lead to blowing snow with widespread snow showers across the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observation at PATO: 8:53am W 7G25 and ended at 12:53pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low tracked into Prince William Sound on Dec 12 as it rotated around a deep upper-level trough centered over Western Alaska. Snow spread west from the Copper Basin as the trough became northwest-southeast oriented. Out west, the upper-level trough contributed to a strong push of cold air across the Bering Sea, which lead to blowing snow with widespread snow showers across the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whittier MAWS reported visibility one half mile or lower with winds gusting in excess of 31 knots (36 mph) on the morning of December 12 through shortly after noon. Similar visibilities were observed at Portage Glacier during the same time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low tracked into Prince William Sound on Dec 12 as it rotated around a deep upper-level trough centered over Western Alaska. Snow spread west from the Copper Basin as the trough became northwest-southeast oriented. Out west, the upper-level trough contributed to a strong push of cold air across the Bering Sea, which lead to blowing snow with widespread snow showers across the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cold Bay ASOS reported visibility one half mile or less with winds gusting as high as 45 knots (52 mph) during a period of snow showers during the afternoon of December 12. Blizzard-like conditions were observed at times in Cold Bay and King Cove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the northern Bering Sea resulting in heavy snow, strong winds, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions to areas along the West Coast from the Y-K Delta to the Bering Sea Coast and St. Lawrence Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gambell Marine Exchange C-MAN station (GBXA2) reported high winds in excess of 60 mph. A peak wind gust of 83 mph was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low tracked into Prince William Sound on Dec 12 as it rotated around a deep upper-level trough centered over Western Alaska. Snow spread west from the Copper Basin as the trough became northwest-southeast oriented. Out west, the upper-level trough contributed to a strong push of cold air across the Bering Sea, which lead to blowing snow with widespread snow showers across the Alaska Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Convective snow showers induced by cold air passing over the relatively warm sea surface temperatures of the Bering Sea led to a five hour period of blizzard conditions recorded at the Port Heiden AWOS on December 13, during which winds gusted as high as 37 knots (43 mph). This was preceded by intermittent periods of blizzard-like conditions on December 12 and into the morning of December 13, caused by the showery nature of the snowfall. Observations to the north and south along the Alaska Peninsula did not show visibility drop below 3/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the northern Bering Sea resulting in heavy snow, strong winds, blowing snow, and blizzard conditions to areas along the West Coast from the Y-K Delta to the Bering Sea Coast and St. Lawrence Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Teller (PATE) AWOS reported wind gusts of up to 64 mph. Blizzard conditions accompanied the strong winds for a number of hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong fronts pushed east across Northern Alaska. Heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions accompanied these fronts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Teller (PATE) AWOS reported blizzard conditions for a number of hours. In addition to the blizzard conditions, wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported, with a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH SLOPES OF THE CENTRAL BROOKS RANGE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moving north across the Interior and Brooks Range produced heavy snow across the southern slopes of the Brooks Range and portions of the Upper Koyukuk Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wiseman (WSMA2) COOP observer reported a 24 hour snowfall amount of 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moving north across the Interior and Brooks Range produced heavy snow across the southern slopes of the Brooks Range and portions of the Upper Koyukuk Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Transportation reported between 6 and 8 inches of snow on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moving north across the Interior and Brooks Range produced heavy snow across the southern slopes of the Brooks Range and portions of the Upper Koyukuk Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Transportation reported 9 inches of snowfall at 6 am at Atigun Pass with another 3 inches of snow by noon, for a storm total of 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather front pushed eastward across the Seward Peninsula and produced strong and gusty winds, as well as reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nome (PAOM) ASOS reported wind gusts up to 61 mph. A Facebook video showed high winds severing Christmas lights in downtown Nome.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong fronts pushed east across Northern Alaska. Heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions accompanied these fronts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kivalina (PAVL) ASOS reported two separate periods of blizzard conditions, where visibility dropped below a quarter of a mile. In addition to the blizzard conditions, wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported, with a peak wind gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong fronts pushed east across Northern Alaska. Heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions accompanied these fronts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brevig Mission (PFKT) AWOS reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph with a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong fronts pushed east across Northern Alaska. Heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions accompanied these fronts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wales (PAIW) AWOS reported blizzard conditions for a number of hours. In addition to the blizzard conditions, wind gusts of up to 59 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong fronts pushed east across Northern Alaska. Heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions accompanied these fronts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fort Yukon (FYNA2) SNOTEL reported a snow depth increase of 6 inches over a 24 hour period. Estimated daily snowfall of 8 inches with a storm total of 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another low in a series of systems affecting the panhandle brought wind to the south and elevation snow to the north as a front tracked northward across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Christmas morning at about 9am, Klondike Highway DOT reported 7 to 12 inches of snow between mile post 8 and the border. The following morning DOT reported another 5 to 8 inches for a storm total of up to 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another low in a series of systems affecting the panhandle brought wind to the south and elevation snow to the north as a front tracked northward across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Haines Highway DOT estimated 6 to 12 inches of snow between mile post 20 and the border by early in the morning of the 26th. Haines Customs COOP reported rain on Christmas morning, then measured 12 inches of snowfall by 7am on the 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another low in a series of systems affecting the panhandle brought wind to the south and elevation snow to the north as a front tracked northward across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A front advancing northward brought high winds to western Prince of Wales Island on Christmas morning. NWS station in Craig measured the first high wind gust of 63 mph at 624am and the Hydaburg Seaplane base AWOS recorded 3 hours of high wind, peaking at 67 mph at 1102am.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS AND HIGH TERRAIN SOUTH OF THE YUKON RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong fronts pushed east across Northern Alaska. Heavy snow, strong winds, and blizzard conditions accompanied these fronts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upper Nome Creek (UCNA2) SNOTEL reported two bursts of heavy snowfall.  Snow depth increased 11 inches.  Estimated snowfall of 8 inches in a 24 hour period, with a storm total of 12 to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force front moved into the region, bringing wind, rain, and upper elevation snow to the Haines and Klondike Highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Klondike Highway was closed due to heavy snow and equipment failures. Skagway Police relayed later to the public that, due to considerable snowfall on the pass, avalanche danger is high and it is not safe for DOT to work in the dark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another low in a series of systems affecting the panhandle brought wind to the south and elevation snow to the north as a front tracked northward across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the late afternoon of Christmas eve, then by about 9am Christmas morning, Klondike Highway DOT reported 7 to 12 inches of snow between mile post 8 and the border. The following morning DOT reported another 5 to 8 inches for a storm total of up to 20 inches. As the storm was moving out, a southerly surge of winds caused a peak wind gust of 62mph at the Skagway airport, measured at 905am on the 26th, and likely causing blowing and drifting snow up the highway. It was reported later by DOT that the Klondike Highway was closed on the Canadian sides due to white out conditions between the summit and Fraser. The US side was closed later as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Taku winds set up over downtown Juneau. The event was frontal driven as a gale to storm force low moved up the coast from just west of Haida Gwaii.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mesonet stations across downtown Juneau measured peak gusts in excess of 60mph most of the daytime on the 27th. The highest wind gust was measured at the South Douglas Harbor profiler, topping out at 87 mph at 1126am. The ANG dock saw a peak gust of 77 mph at 103pm. The following week a resident of the Mendenhall Valley reported that two trees came down on her property from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moved into the Gulf of Alaska between 12/5 and 12/7 with fairly cold air in place with higher pressure over Southcentral Alaska out ahead of this system.  This helped produce strong, gusty and offshore winds that resulted in localized blowing snow and blizzard conditions through Thompson Pass.  Very strong northwesterly flow also developed on the western side of this low as it lifted north near the Alaska Peninsula, with high wind gusts up to 75 mph recorded in the city of Kodiak on 12/5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Thompson Pass mesonet station recorded gusts continuously over 35 mph between the afternoon of 12/5 and the evening of 12/7.  This was coincident with temperatures well below freezing and steady, falling snow.  This event followed a more minor period of blowing and drifting snow through Thompson Pass as reported by the Department of Transportation early on the morning of 12/4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moved into the Gulf of Alaska between 12/5 and 12/7 with fairly cold air in place with higher pressure over Southcentral Alaska out ahead of this system.  This helped produce strong, gusty and offshore winds that resulted in localized blowing snow and blizzard conditions through Thompson Pass.  Very strong northwesterly flow also developed on the western side of this low as it lifted north near the Alaska Peninsula, with high wind gusts up to 75 mph recorded in the city of Kodiak on 12/5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kodiak ASOS reported a peak wind gust of 75 mph at 7 PM AKST 12/5.  A nearby NOS site also reported gusts from the northwest between 60 and 75 mph between about 5 PM and 9 PM AKST 12/5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively-tilted trough lifted across Southcentral Alaska between December 7th and 8th. The accompanying front produced a band of moderate to heavy snow which pushed north up the Kenai Peninsula, reaching Anchorage on the evening of Dec 7. The front passed through Anchorage around midnight, leading to a sharp end in precipitation.  Several inches of snow fell across much of the rest of Southcentral Alaska with this frontal system as it moved past, ranging from mostly 6 to 13 inches for much of the Kenai Peninsula out to the Matanuska Valley, as well as the Prince William Sound area.||Meanwhile, the parent surface low of the front responsible for the snowfall event across Southcentral moved into the Kuskokwim Delta between December 7th and 8th, producing areas of snow, blowing snow and reduced visibility along the Kuskokwim Delta Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts during an intense round of snow that pushed up the Kenai Peninsula into Anchorage late December 7th to the morning of December 8th ranged from 4 to 10 inches across town.  The highest storm total snowfall of 10 inches was reported on the Anchorage Hillside at around 7 AM AKST 12/8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved north from the North Pacific toward southcentral Alaska on 12/9 through 12/13 as the system, which consisted of a series of low pressure systems brought repeating periods of heavy snow and winds to the region extending from the Kenai Peninsula into the greater Prince William Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From AK 511 (DOT) reported that the Richardson Hwy from MP 30 to MP 42 as of 6:56AM 12/11/23 had very difficult travel conditions with blowing and drifting snow, icy patches and packed snow on roadways. The DOT warned of possible road closure with travel not advised. Snow of 6 to 10 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system comprised of two waves moving north, brought high winds across the panhandle. The second wave was stronger, and was responsible for the bulk of the high winds. Accumulating snow was also recorded in the northern panhandle. A long fetch of moisture accompanied this system and high astronomical tides during the period of high winds resulted in minor coastal flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Klondike Highway DOT reported around 3 inches of snow and drifting snow with difficult travel conditions at around 10 am on the 11th near the upper elevations of the highway. Near sea level, rain was falling with strong wind gusts out of the south at 35-55mph. These conditions continued through noon on the 12th when winds shifted to a light northerly and Skagway saw a change to snow with heavy rates. By 511pm, the Klondike Highway report was updated to report very difficult travel conditions and a range of 8 to 16 inches between mile post 8 to the border. The highway was closed for the remainder of the night at 838pm as reported by Skagway Police. A wind shift back to the south changed snow back to rain near sea level then higher speeds helped push the system out, but with blowing snow up the road. The COOP in Skagway reported 6 inches the morning of the 12th and another 5.5 inches the morning of the 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overrunning precipitation fell over the Chilkat Valley as cold air was maintained by northwest wind. The southern portions of the Haines Highway and Haines saw an extended period of freezing rain with while the border at elevation saw significant snow accumulations. The freezing level was midway up the terrain for the the Klondike highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell through the morning of the 29th in Haines with a quarter inch of accumulation being reported by 1130am. The freezing rain changed to a period of moderate snow through the afternoon, then back to moderate freezing rain before temperatures slowly warmed above freezing near sea level. The following morning, Haines Police reported a power line down on McPheters Road and photos were shared on social media of tree damage all over town. Meanwhile, snow had continued out the highway where 7.3 inches fell between 330-830pm on the 29th and Haines Customs COOP measured 17 inches by 7am on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overrunning precipitation fell over the Chilkat Valley as cold air was maintained by northwest wind. The southern portions of the Haines Highway and Haines saw an extended period of freezing rain with while the border at elevation saw significant snow accumulations. The freezing level was midway up the terrain for the the Klondike highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and freezing rain fell through the afternoon of the 29th to the following morning around Skagway and Dyea. DOT reported 6-10 inches of snow between mile 8 and and the border on the Klondike Highway the morning of the 30th. Skagway Police issued a notice to the public the following morning as well, noting conditions on roads remained slick and encouraged all drivers to exercise extreme caution due to vehicle accidents including one at 2.3 mile Dyea Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An RWIS/DOT observation site in Portage reported 2 to 3 hours of moderate to heavy snow with visibility under 1 mile between 9 PM AKST 12/7 and 12 AM ASKST 12/8 with winds gusting from the west between 20 and 35 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Matanuska Valley saw the heaviest snow on the evening of Dec 7 until shortly after midnight with a frontal passage. 3-6 inches of snow was observed, with the highest total of 6 inches reported from a trained spotter in Sutton at 7 AM AKST December 8th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Homer Police department around 1:15PM Dec. 10th. They said once you get past Anchor Point, the wind blows about 30 mph between Anchor Point into Homer. They said there are Whiteout conditions and snowing pretty good in Homer right now. The same was described for the Homer Bluffs when asked. Ob at PAHO as of 1:00PM was 2SM -SN 06014G27 OVC014. From AK 511 East End Road from Pioneer Ave to MP 20 as of 4:36AM 12/11/23: Overall Road Status: Difficult. Surface conditions: Drifting snow, snow on roadway, wet and icy roads. Atmospheric conditions: Rain/Snow mix. Warnings: Slippery road surface. Snow (inches): 1-10 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow with visibilities below 1/4 were reported along Seward Highway and the Whittier Access Road.  Alaska 511 reported extremely difficult road conditions due to the heavy snow with observations showing the reduced visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved north from the North Pacific toward southcentral Alaska on 12/9 through 12/13 as the system, which consisted of a series of low pressure systems brought repeating periods of heavy snow and winds to the region extending from the Kenai Peninsula into the greater Prince William Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snow reduced visibilities to near zero along Seward Highway near Turnagain Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved north from the North Pacific toward southcentral Alaska on 12/9 through 12/13 as the system, which consisted of a series of low pressure systems brought repeating periods of heavy snow and winds to the region extending from the Kenai Peninsula into the greater Prince William Sound.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow would impact the Richardson Highway, closing the highway due to heavy snow and poor visibilities.  Highway department reported 6-17 of snowfall with avalanches at MP 39 and 50.5.  COOP observer in Valdez measured 11 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low moved through the gulf slowed down and began to weaken, sending a front northward through the panhandle as it did so. Cold air was confined to the northern panhandle along with northerly winds, which resulted in parts of the Haines Highway receiving significant snow accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell through the night of the 3rd into the 4th with just over 5 inches by morning COOP reports from Haines town area and at the customs station. Snowfall rates significantly increased through mid day, causing Haines Police to send a notice that road conditions were very poor and travel was not advised. Snow continued into the evening, totaling 8 to 10 inches in town before changing to rain with a short period of freezing rain. Near the border, another round of heavy snow lasted through the morning of the 5th, giving the COOP station there another 24 hour total of 17 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force front moved into the region, bringing wind, rain, and upper elevation snow to the Haines and Klondike Highways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the early morning hours of the 14th with 7-9 inches accumulating along the Chilkat Peninsula by 7-8am, and 12 inches measured at Haines Customs. The Haines Pass was closed by 832am and Haines Police advised the public of poor driving conditions. Snow then changed to rain in town with a wind shift to the south and gusts of 25-35 mph. These warmer temperatures and wind worked their way up the highway, likely resulting in an avalanche occurring near Klukwan at 1031am and a tree across the highway at 30 mile. Heavy snow near the border lasted into the afternoon with another 9 inches measured at the Customs station the following morning, for a storm total of 21 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grouse Creek Divide SNOTEL recorded an increase in snow depth of 10.7 inches between the afternoon of 12/7 and about 11 PM of the same evening.  Heavy snow and blowing snow was also recorded intermittently in Seward and Whittier during this timeframe.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer reported 13 inches of new snowfall in Valdez from heavy snow moving through between the late evening of 12/7 and the morning of 12/8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same winter storm which impacted the zones to the south and east of Anchorage would move into the Cook Inlet on the 10th of December causing widespread areas of blowing snow due to snowfall of 2-3 inches and winds which gusted to 50-60 mph overnight on 12/11 across the Anchorage Bowl and heavier snows across the Susitna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter report from Potter Valley of blowing snow and winds gusting to 60 mph (time based on nearby sensor gusts).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same winter storm which impacted the zones to the south and east of Anchorage would move into the Cook Inlet on the 10th of December causing widespread areas of blowing snow due to snowfall of 2-3 inches and winds which gusted to 50-60 mph overnight on 12/11 across the Anchorage Bowl and heavier snows across the Susitna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 10.5 inches was reported at Trapper Creek, AK.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking toward south-central Alaska swept a front from south to north over the panhandle on the 23rd. This front brought heavy snow to the upper elevations of the Haines and Klondike Highways and high wind to the southern half of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter south of Haines on the Chilkat Peninsula reported 6 inches of snow falling between 6-9am on the 23rd and another 5.5 inches by 3pm when it was changing to rain, for a storm total of 11.5 inches. In town, 16 inches were measured shortly after 4pm. A strong wind shift to the south with gusts of 25-40mph and a change to rain occurred, possibly resulting in a rock slide reported at 730pm by the Haines Police, which made the Haines Highway impassible at 15 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system comprised of two waves moving north, brought high winds across the panhandle. The second wave was stronger, and was responsible for the bulk of the high winds. Accumulating snow was also recorded in the northern panhandle. A long fetch of moisture accompanied this system and high astronomical tides during the period of high winds resulted in minor coastal flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong low advancing out of the S brought multiple wind gusts near 60 mph over an extended duration of time. A peak wind gust of 60 mph was measured at the Yakutat Airport ASOS at 6:08 AM AKST on December 12.

Accumulating snow was also recorded in the northern panhandle. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two advancing waves brought two periods of high winds for the Ketchikan area. Multiple sites across Ketchikan reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph, with the highest wind gust of 76 mph reported at the Saxman Marine Exchange site at 713 PM AKST. A utility company also reported a power outage near Point Higgins road at 639 PM AKST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mekoryuk AWOS recorded several hours of snow and blowing snow between 1 PM and 10 PM AKST 12/7 with visibility as low as 1/4 mile and sustained winds between 25 and 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking toward south-central Alaska swept a front from south to north over the panhandle on the 23rd. This front brought heavy snow to the upper elevations of the Haines and Klondike Highways and high wind to the southern half of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began early on the 23rd with rates becoming heavy for several hours in the middle of the day. A social media report and call to the office noted 8-10 inches by about 9pm when it was changing to rain. The following morning, DOT reported up to 12 inches had fallen along the Klondike Highway near the border and very difficult road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system comprised of two waves moving north, brought high winds across the panhandle. The second wave was stronger, and was responsible for the bulk of the high winds. Accumulating snow was also recorded in the northern panhandle. A long fetch of moisture accompanied this system and high astronomical tides during the period of high winds resulted in minor coastal flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate waves resulted in two periods of windy conditions for Juneau, with the second, during the late afternoon and evening of December 12, being the strongest. The first wave in the morning saw high winds at elevation sites and caused multiple reports of downed trees and power outages out the road from Lena Point north. The Forest Service closed access to the Otter Way parking area due to fallen trees and unstable soil conditions. Peak wind at the Juneau airport of 66 mph was measured at 516pm local time. The AML dock downtown had a peak gust of 63mph in the same hour. Broken power poles and outages continued into the evening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration wind event saw the Sitka ASOS reporting wind gusts approaching high winds through much of the day, with a peak during the early evening the Sitka Airport measuring a gust of 63 mph at 657 PM AKST. Social media reports later mentioned at least two trees down on houses, including on Halibut Point Rd. Earlier in the day around the noon hour, local law enforcement reported a boat adrift and some items being washed away. Wind gusts of around 45 mph were occurring at the time of peak high astronomical tide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a downed tree at approximately 130pm, in the power line at 5.5 mile Zimovia Highway and another tree town at 6.5 mile. Trained spotter also reported the outage. Winds on nearby Zarembo Island saw a peak gust of 68 mph at 255pm. A second power outage was reported at 320pm with a tree down across 8 mile Zimovia Highway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm brought wind gusts of 58 mph measured by the AWOS at the Metlakatla Seaplane Base at 903am on the 12th. At 623pm, an NWS Employee reported a wind gust of 61 mph at the Annette WSO. Later in the evening, a utility company reported power line and pole damage on Walden Point Road in relation to the gusty conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate waves brought periods of windy conditions to Prince of Wales Island, with the second wave resulting in a power outage which affected Hollis and Klawock. A gust of 54 mph was observed by the Thorne Bay RAWS site at 254pm and the FAA webcam at Kasaan saw sustained winds over 40 mph between 1 to 3pm. The Klawock ASOS measured a peak gust of 48 mph at 519pm and the Hydaburg AWOS was down at the time.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN ALASKA RANGE NORTH OF TRIMS CAMP
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather front brought strong winds as it moved north over the Alaska Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds in the Alaska Range began ramping up the afternoon of the 14th. The Texas Condo mesonet site reported gusts as high as 75 mph during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a 988 mb low over the eastern Beaufort Sea moved over the Arctic Coast of Alaska Bringing strong winds, heavy snow and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at Point Thomson the afternoon of the 20th. Wind gusts as high as 47 mph were reported by the automated weather station. By late evening, the visibility improved as the rate of snow decreased. Strong winds continued into the morning of the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On October 23, 2023 a 996 mb area of low pressure moved northward over the Chukotsk Peninsula of eastern Russia. A series of fronts associated with this low traveled north along the west coast of Alaska bringing a combination of strong winds, heavy snow and elevated surf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the upper Kobuk Valley as the fronts moved across. The Dahl Creek Snotel station reported 7 inches of snow in the 24 hour period beginning at 5 AM on October 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On October 23, 2023 a 996 mb area of low pressure moved northward over the Chukotsk Peninsula of eastern Russia. A series of fronts associated with this low traveled north along the west coast of Alaska bringing a combination of strong winds, heavy snow and elevated surf.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the front moved over the western Arctic Coast, it brought a mixture of rain and snow as well as high winds. Cape Lisburne saw gusts as high as 93 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed over the Bering Strait Coast as a front moved to the north over the area. Wales reported a gust to 63 mph, while Tin City reported a gust to 59 mph and Teller reported a gust to 54 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the Noatak Valley as the fronts moved northward over the area. The Kelly Station reported 7 inches of snow in a 24 hour period beginning at 4 AM on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Since 2011, Suicide Basin, a side valley that is dammed by the Mendenhall Glacier, fills with water from snowmelt and rainfall then drains during the summer as a glacial outburst flood. This year, the glacier dammed lake in Suicide Basin, like in 3 other years (2018, 2019 and 2020), was full to the top of the Mendenhall Glacier (top of the ice dam) and started to flow down a side drainage channel. After about 7 days, Suicide Basin began to drain sub-glacially. Mendenhall Lake started to rise from the additional water from the basin Thursday evening into the early morning of Friday, August 4th. In the morning hours of Friday, August 4th visual reports from the USGS Mendenhall Lake gage and webcam confirmed that the basin had started to drain. The Mendenhall Lake gage steadily rose through Friday, then the rate of rise increased exponentially as the inflow to the lake from Suicide Basin was getting larger. Over the course of this glacier dam outburst flooding event, Mendenhall Lake and River rose an impressive nine and a half feet over less than two days, breaking all previous records, and causing significant impacts. This flooding event was historic due to areas seeing flooding that usually do not flood along with the severity of the flooding and the rate of riverbank erosion. There were moderate to major flooding impacts in the Mendenhall Valley, where a majority of Juneau���s population resides, and historically does not flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The level of Mendenhall Lake was near normal levels of 5.5 feet before this event started, late Thursday August 3rd, and by Saturday morning water levels were over moderate flood stage of 10 feet. By this time areas near Skaters Cabin Rd, View Dr, and the Mendenhall Glacier Visitor Center were experiencing moderate flooding impacts with the Mendenhall Lake campground being evacuated. By midday on Saturday, the water levels exceeded the previous record from 2016 of 11.99 feet and impacts were increasing along the Mendenhall River. Homes along View Dr were cut off due to the significant amount of flooding over the road along with flood waters from 1 to 3 feet deep. Areas below the Mendenhall Back Loop Road bridge started to see flooding of yards and increasing bank erosion in the afternoon of August 5th.| |The river continued to rise at a fast pace through the day into the evening hours and the City of Juneau then set up their Emergency Operations Center. In the evening hours, many areas (roads and homes) below Mendenhall Back Loop Road bridge along the Mendenhall River were experiencing moderate flooding along with significant bank erosion of 50 to 200 feet. From that bank erosion one home fell into the river with another large 3 story condominium building was evacuated as it became compromised and could also fall into the river. Going into the late evening hours a portion of another house fell into the river with many other homes along the river being undermined and at risk of falling into the river.  Homes and roads around Meander Way, Marion Dr, River Court and Killewich Rd flooded with moderate impacts in the late evening and the local electric company cut power to those residences due to flood waters being 2 to 4 feet deep. ||The river crested near 14.97 feet (preliminary crest from the USGS) around 11:30 PM AKDT on August 5th. At the peak of the event there was 3 to 7 feet of water in and around homes along View Dr. The Mendenhall Lake and River were back below minor flood stage of 9.0 feet by 9:45 AM AKDT on Sunday, August 6th.||While other locations in the Mendenhall Valley, above Mendenhall Back Loop Road bridge, have experienced flooding impacts from these types of events in the past, they saw major damage this time around. This event was 3 feet higher than the previous record from 2016 and the Mendenhall Lake and River rose approximately 9.42 feet.||In the end there were about 48 homes affected with 13 destroyed, 8 with major damage and 7 with minor. This historic event saw the largest amount of water released from Suicide Basin in a single event and approximately 14 billion gallons of water came out of the basin and into the Mendenhall Lake and River system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another early season winter system impacted parts of the southern Copper River Basin on October 5th as a low curving northwest across the Gulf interacted with a cold air mass in place over the interior valleys across Southcentral Alaska.  Warm air overrunning this air mass resulted in a temperature profile favorable for freezing rain and sleet as precipitation overspread the Copper River Basin, with numerous reports of slush, sleet and ice buildup along parts of the Glenn Highway and southern half of the Richardson Highway through the night of 10/5.  The worst conditions and ice/sleet accumulations were reported along the Richardson Highway and Edgerton Highway north of Thompson Pass, where around 1/4 to 1/2 inches of ice and sleet accumulation occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the morning of 10/6, NWS Anchorage made calls out to the Tazlina Department of Transportation and Edgerton Department of Transportation to assess conditions after a prolonged period of mixed precipitation.  Both locations reported that freezing rain started around 2 pm AKST on 10/5 and persisted well into the night. Tazlina estimated 1/2 inch of ice/sleet accumulation on the Richardson Highway.  An estimated 1/4 inch to 1/2 inch of ice/sleet accumulation was reported on Edgerton Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough lifting north from the Gulf approached the Copper River Basin on the evening of 10/12 as a shield of precipitation spread north ahead of the trough axis from the Gulf coast into the southern Copper River Basin.  Numerical models were slow to catch on to the amount of cold air holding on in place over the basin, and many solutions depicted a quick transition from snow to rain shortly after precipitation onset as warmer air began to filter in with southeast winds ahead of the approaching trough.  Instead, colder air than expected held on away from the coastline for most of the night of 10/12, lasting into the early morning hours of 10/13.  Up to 11 inches of new snowfall was reported along parts of the Edgerton Highway near Kenny Lake by the early morning of 10/13.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alaska Department of Transportation reported at 4:50 AKST that up to 11 inches of snow had fallen overnight along portions of the Edgerton Highway near Kenny Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of steady, mixed precipitation overspread much of Southwest Alaska as a warm nose of temperatures above freezing spread in from the south while a shallow layer of temperatures below freezing held in place at the surface across much of the Lower Kuskokwim Valley.  This temperature profile allowed periods of rain, freezing rain and snow to last for several hours between the late evening of 10/15 into the morning of 10/16, including over Aniak and Sleetmute.  Freezing rain and slick conditions were reported at Sleetmute on the morning of 10/16, but no accumulation reports or estimates were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain accumulation was reported by the school in Sleetmute at 7:50 AM AKST 10/16. Apparent freezing rain also showed up on nearby webcams and as unknown precipitation in METARs for several hours in Aniak, Kalskag and Sleetmute. Slippery ground conditions were reported as kids walked to school in Sleetmute on the morning of 10/16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure and frontal system lifting across the Alaska Gulf helped push areas of light precipitation across the greater Anchorage area on the morning of 10/29.  Surface temperatures under a low level inversion were initially cold enough for precipitation to mainly fall as freezing rain/drizzle across parts of Anchorage, resulting in slick road conditions and up to 0.1 inches of ice accumulation across town until temperatures warmed above freezing later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Anchorage employee reported 0.1 of ice accumulation during the morning of 10/29.  Several other locations reported freezing rain/drizzle resulting in very slick road conditions and cars sliding off roadways between Anchorage and Eagle River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moving north over the Alaska Range brought freezing rain to the Upper Tanana Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain was reported at several locations in the Upper Tanana Valley the evening of the 5th. 0.125 inches of ice accumulated on flat surfaces at Robertson River as reported by the NWS COOP Observer. The automated weather station at Northway reported a mixture of rain, freezing rain and ice pellets during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season heavy snow event recharged the snow cover in the Copper River Basin while early June warm temperatures created a new snowmelt-induced flood on the Moose Creek in Glennallen, AK.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Late season snow in the Moose Creek Basin rapidly melted at the beginning of June 2023 causing another episode of flooding in the Glennallen area.  While not as severe as the May flood event, the June event flooded the Glennallen Library and closed Glenn Highway due to highwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While most of Southcentral had a very slow start to the fire season due to a persistent, abnormally cool and wet pattern, the Matanuska Valley and parts of the Copper River Basin became a locally drier and warmer spots by the beginning of June.  Strong southeast flow and drying/warming off the terrain of the Chugach Range resulted in (pre-greenup) Red Flag conditions for several hours during the afternoon across portions of the Matanuska Valley and Copper River Basin on 6/5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Borderline Red Flag conditions at the Gulkana ASOS began at 2:53 pm AKST and continued through 6:00 pm AKST. Temperatures ranged from 70 to 72 Fahrenheit. South winds ranged from 10 to 17 mph with a peak gust of 24 mph. Relative humidity ranged from 18 to 22%. A few other nearby mesonet observations reported temperatures between 70 and 75 between 2:30 pm AKST and 7:30 pm AKST with winds around 15 mph and relative humidity values of 18 to 22%.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure developed over Canada and spread into Southcentral near the end of July 2023.  Recent wet, cool and cloudy conditions over the region quickly snapped to sunny and warm weather as the high pressure moved into place, resulting in a large release of water vapor from evaporation from wet soils and transpiration from plants/trees.  A cap of warm, dry air coupled with high temperatures and moisture near the surface allowed large amounts of instability to build up through the afternoon of 7/24, with thunderstorms with frequent lightning developing as the cap broke.  Most of the lightning activity occurred where fuels were wetter on the north side of the Copper Basin, but a late night thunderstorm with fairly frequent lightning eventually was able to move off of the Wrangell Mountains into Glennallen and Gulkana where fuels were much drier near 12 AM AKST 7/25.  Fortunately, no new fire starts were recorded despite the ample lightning activity observed for most of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Global Lightning Detection Network and daily lightning summary from the Alaska Inter-agency Coordination Center and Bureau of Land Management showed numerous lightning strikes across much of the Copper River Basin during the afternoon and evening of 7/24. This lightning occurred for several hours in intense thunderstorms observed on satellite and radar, with one thunderstorm cell moving into the area of greater concern for drier fire fuels over Glennallen and Gulkana late at night on 7/24, or right around 12 am AKST 7/25.  In spite of this, no new fire starts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While most of Southcentral had a very slow start to the fire season due to a persistent, abnormally cool and wet pattern, the Matanuska Valley and parts of the Copper River Basin became a locally drier and warmer spots by the beginning of June.  Strong southeast flow and drying/warming off the terrain of the Chugach Range resulted in (pre-greenup) Red Flag conditions for several hours during the afternoon across portions of the Matanuska Valley and Copper River Basin on 6/5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Red Flag Warning (pre-greenup) criteria at the Palmer ASOS began at 2:15 pm AKST and continued through 6:00 pm AKST. Temperatues ranged from 72 to 74 Fahrenheit. Southeast winds ranged from 15 mph to 25 mph with a peak gust of 33 mph. Relative humidity ranged from 22% to 25% during this same time period.
